target: October and November are the months when the dread disease, hog cholera, is most likely to appear. During those months and until snow covers the ground swine raisers are urged by the United States Department of Agriculture to be especially observing when feeding hogs in the morning. Any animals in the herd which fail to come to their feed, and particularly those having arched backs and rough coats, should be removed promptly from the rest of the herd.

input:
correct: As the sun was slowly sinking
in the deep bosom of the ocean the
beautiful steamer Corcoran movea
out into the channel of the Poto-
mac and like a thing of life wend-
ed her way to Marshall Hall the
eden of pleasure. For certainly no
spot along the line is more beau-
uful. In honor be it said that the
management was thorough in de-
tail. Hach officer stood at his post
and performed his duty well.
Every man manifested aninterest
in the excursionists and no one of
the five hundred persons who
went down the river regrets that
he went. For the unanimous
verdict was that it was the best
of the best of the season. The
choruses of the Glee were simply
immense. The voices one by one
were rich and musical. One
would searcely know that these
were the same voices that often
times have been styled the pride
of the town so vast was the im-
provement. In fine everything
was superb.
target: As the sun was slowly sinking in the deep bosom of the ocean the beautiful steamer Corcoran moved out into the channel of the Potomac and like a thing of life wended her way to Marshall Hall the eden of pleasure. For certainly no spot along the line is more beautiful. In honor be it said that the management was thorough in detail. Each officer stood at his post and performed his duty well. Every man manifested an interest in the excursionists and no one of the five hundred persons who went down the river regrets that he went. For the unanimous verdict was that it was the best of the best of the season. The choruses of the Glee were simply immense. The voices one by one were rich and musical. One would scarcely know that these were the same voices that often times have been styled the pride of the town so vast was the improvement. In fine everything was superb.

target: The time spent by the parties in kicking, over this Temperance movement, is time thrown away. Prohibitionists are free born, like other men, and have a right to vote as they please. It is a fact, fully settled with the temperance men in this city, that hereafter each year, an anti-license ticket will be put in the field by some party or parties, and as the present movement has excited so much opposition and so much abuse for many who "pray" Temperance, and from whom we ought to expect help, I, for one am in favor of carrying this independent movement into the county election next fall. There is no law in earth or Heaven that compels so many of our citizens to vote a whisky ticket. We demand recognition in the control of our city and county affairs and we propose to have it. DELTA.

target: Take a chicken trussed for boiling; rub it over with lemon juice; place a piece of slitted fat bacon on the breast, tie it on; wrap the chicken in a well-buttered cloth, put it into a stevpan with sufficient boiling water to cover, with three or four sliced onions, a bunch of herbs, about 12 black and white peppercorns, and enough salt to season; bring to the boil, and simmer for 40 to 60 minutes, according to the size of the fowl. Then take out, remove the cloth and skin, and dish up on a bed of steamed rice. Garnish with parsley and button mushrooms; serve the chicken for dinner while hot, with cheese cream sauce.

target: When he reached Tembual he un-loaded his stock, opened his kegs of liquor, and which no trader had dared at that time to open an Indian business. But he found his venture likely to prove an unprofitable one. The natives did not want hats; they wanted blankets and beads, and looking-glasses, and, above all, liquor, but they looked askance at hats. Then an idea occurred to De Soto. He wanted to introduce those hats. His simple expedient was to refuse to sell anything to a Kaffir unless he bought a hat. The Kaffirs wanted the goods, so they bought the hats.

target: When a Kaffir buys anything, he feels bound to make use of it. The natives, therefore, donned their head-gear and returned to their kraals. Now appeared the brilliancy of the trader's idea. Fashion rules the world. It is as strong in Africa as in America, and when those who stayed at home saw the travelers return in all the glory of this strange covering they felt behindhand and old-fashioned. Their desire to possess the latest thing in hats became intense. They paid De Villiers a visit, and his stock no longer hung heavy on his hands. The hats were soon sold.

target: Mr. Taylor's resignation has been deeply regretted by those interested in the success of the Y. M. C. A. branch which came here in 1905 when the association was housed in rented property in North California street, and there were only 17 members. Doctor Hummons was president of the board of management when Mr. Taylor came here. During the 11 years Mr. Taylor has been here the membership reached 1,000. Mr. Taylor said he attributes much of the success of the association to the interest of George L. Knox, now president of the board of management. In speaking of the new building erected two years ago when the new building was dedicated, Booker T. Washington said his work was not surprised by that of any like organization he knew of, and that it was filling one of the greatest needs in the community of the city.

target: Seismographs to Find Batteries. Among the many things to which science has turned its attention and abilities since the beginning of the European war, none, perhaps, has been of greater importance than the development of some means by which men in the trenches could locate with fair accuracy the position of an opposing battery or group of machine guns. This matter has been worked on in many ways, but the nearest science ever came to solving the proposition was the use of strong glasses with the aid of observation balloons and aeroplanes.

target: Now, however, a new development has been announced, in which the seismograph, long known as an earthquake detector, is made use of. It is declared to be possible to tell from the shock of the gunfire just how large the guns are, what their distance away is, and the exact direction in which they lie. A practical eye, it is claimed, can tell not only these points, but can distinguish between the shock of firing and the shock of a missile striking the ground, and also is able to determine just how many guns compose any battery that may be in action.

As many as 750 pictures may be made without recharging, and any number of these may be withdrawn for development.

target: Nearly all roots and underground stems that are eaten as vegetables contain large quantities of water, in addition to starch or some similar material, a little nitrogenous matter, and mineral matter. The roots and stems store materials for future growth during favorable seasons. Plants that store most of their food as insoluble starch (as potatoes and cassava) are known as "starch-bearing plants," while those which store much of their food in the form of soluble carbohydrates (sugar in the case of the beet) which give the root a more or less juicy character are classed as "succulent roots."

target: Rounding a bend in the river we pass a number of shipbuilding yards and factories, and then, as our steamer draws up to her anchorage, the whole of the town is suddenly revealed to our view like the unrolling of a scene in a panorama. Backed by low hills, the city lies on a piece of level ground in an open and beautiful valley, stretching for several miles by the river's side. At no point more than a mile and a half wide and deep enough for steamers of considerable size. The whole of the waterfront is occupied by jetties and piers, whereon many different types load and discharge their cargoes. The spires of the cathedral and numerous churches stand up, white and gleaming, above the roofs of the houses, fine buildings face the water, and on a wide thoroughfare between them and the river streams of traffic pass to and fro.

target: The chief business in Guayaquil is, of course, the export of cacao, for Ecuador is one of the greatest producers of this valuable food product in the world, the welfare of the city, and, indeed, of the whole country depending almost entirely on the cacao crop and the market. Most of the firms engaged in this trade have their offices and warehouses on or near the Malecon above the Calle Nueve de Octubre, conveniently situated for receiving the product from the river craft that bring it down from the interior and for shipping it away to Europe and America, while the broad streets serve as drying grounds for the reddish-brown beans.

target: Starches from different plants or plant parts differ in the form of the starch grain, so that starches of various kinds can be identified by the aid of the microscope. But from the culinary standpoint they are practically interchangeable, and one form of starch may be substituted for another in nearly all cases. Just as we may substitute the yam or the dish of rice for the potato as a 'vegetable' so we may use starch from the potato, or corn, or wheat, or rice for thickening gravies or making puddings, making slight changes in proportion, according to the expansive powers of each kind.

target: In northern regions the potato is the most important of the starch-bearing plants. In the southern states the sweet potato is more generally used. One reason why the potato has become such a favorite is doubtless its lack of pronounced flavor. Then, too, it is easily grown, gives an abundant yield, and may be readily stored for winter use.

target: Beets contain a larger percentage of sugar than most vegetables, and should be baked or steamed to retain as much of this as possible. Even so, some color and sweetness are lost in the water in which they are cooked. Radishes, red or white, when a little too large to eat raw, may be cooked like turnips and served with a white sauce.

target: The parsnip is said to have been cultivated even before the Christian era. The woody fiber of these roots is softened by freezing without injury to other portions. Hence they are left in the ground until the frost comes or even through the winter. But the roots must be used before they begin to grow again or they lose their sweetness and get "rusty." The larger ones are likely to be less sweet and more woody. Small parsnips left in the ground in the spring will cook in less than a half hour. If steamed in their skins, they lose sweetness than by boiling. They should be peeled after cooking, and served plain or with butter sauce, or sauteed in butter or washed and made into thick fritters. They may be made into a stew with potatoes, onions, and milk.

target: When potatoes are old and wrinkled they are much improved by cutting off the ends or by partially or wholly paring and by soaking in cold water for several hours like dried beans, etc. In fact, inferior potatoes of any age are much improved by paring and soaking. Where potatoes are inexpensive or the parings can be fed to animals it is often a profitable custom to pare before cooking, since thus imperfections and strong-flavored portions are disposed of, leaving a nearly pure starch, comparable to arrowroot or tapioca and ready for the table as soon as cooked; this is true notwithstanding that such cooking causes considerable loss of the nutrients in the potato.

target: A walk through the busier streets show that there is considerable activity in the city in both wholesale and offices and stores, the latter exhibiting splendid stocks of goods behind their big plate-glass windows. Commerce is carried on at the water front and in the streets in the vicinity. The Malecon, one side occupied by large commercial houses and the other open to the river, though not as wide as other streets in the city, has room for three street car lines (one of them electric) and a double railroad track without incommoding the ordinary street traffic. At the river side piers are built out over the green bank, connecting with floating landing stages, where cargo is transferred to small sailing vessels, river steamers, sailing vessels, lighters, and cayucas, to be transferred to the railroad for conveyance to the customhouse or carried to nearby warehouses.

target: Although Guayaquil is only about two degrees south of the equator the climate is very much cooler than might be expected, the dry season, lasting from May to December, being the best part of the year. Visiting the city during the month of August the writer found the days agreeably cool and the nights almost cold; even during the hottest part of the day, from noon to four o'clock, a cloth suit could be worn without any discomfort. This surprising coolness is due to the stream of cold water from the Antarctic regions, that flowing northward along the west coast of South America, makes a considerable reduction in the temperature.

target: Sweet potatoes are not strictly tubers like Irish potatoes, but are tuberous roots. They should be kept in a dry place if possible at a temperature of from 50 to 65 degrees F. Because their sweetness is to some extent lost in water, they are better steamed than boiled, and baking is a favorite method of preparation. After steaming they may be sifted and used in puddings or pies like squash and added to breads, particularly corn-bread.

target: The maple trees of the nation produce 47,000,000 pounds of maple sugar annually—about half a pound for each person. And the demand for the product, it is estimated, is ten times greater than the supply. Despite this fact, statistics show that less than half of the trees available for the purpose are utilized. This means that a good many farmers throughout the country are neglecting to take advantage of an important source of income.

target: A New Vegetable—the Dasheen. A new tuber which has received some attention from the bureau of plant industry is the dasheen from tropical countries. The plants resemble the caladium, which is such a popular ornamental plant, and the taro, which provides the "poi" of the Hawaiians, and which is a staple food in many tropical islands. The dasheens may be served like potatoes, boiled, fried, creamed, etc., but to many are like potatoes, most acceptable when baked. target: After a round of visits to the producers' studios in Hollywood, I came to the great studio of DeMille in Culver City and found them somewhat chagrined because their proposition concerning a moving picture of Negro life had been misinterpreted by the newspapers. It is not their intention to give the history of the race problem nor any solution of the race problem or to deal with Negro-white relations in any particular. What they aim at is a sort of Negro 'Humoresque,' a sympathetic presentation of Negro life with no white characters in it, or at least with no race problems in it.

target: That so merits sympathy for her illness in addition to the praise that the original Negro record artist also deserves, is the fact that she made the disclosure of an incident that occurred in Kansas City some years ago, just after the Tulsa, Okla. -ma riots. Miss Smith happened to be playing in the Missouri town while local charity was soliciting contributions for the distressed refugees from Tulsa. Miss Smith, anxious to donate, sought out the collectors and gave two hundred dollars and sought no publicity. The story was told by Mr. J. C. McLain, who was in Kansas City then but is now a resident of Washington. It is little wonder that she is so materially prosperous. People in public life seldom do such things without an eye to the publicity value of the act.

target: High Street Now a Slum. There are famous characters to meet in memory to be left, and many of one all the way along the Canongate and the old High Street in every close with its narrow entry leading to the "lands" behind—those tall white houses built like fortresses and densely crowded, which held within a smaller area all that is famous and noble and memorable in the history of the burgh. It is a slum now, where tattered garments hang out to dry, where poverty is naked in crowded tenements, from which on days of royal progress, whenever a carriage passes, there swarms out a legion of "barfit bairns" with shrill cheers and fluttering rags. In the old days gone by, the life and fashion and look of Edinburgh were closely housed.

target: There are tens of thousands of day laborers in this country—common workmen—putting their lives into drudgery, who, if they had only been aroused to their possibilities, would have been employers themselves—would have been men of force, of standing in their community—but they have been held down by their ignorance of their own power. They have never discovered themselves, and so they must be "hewers of wood and drawers of water." We see them everywhere—splendid men and women, who impress us as giants in possibility, but who are totally ignorant of the great forces that are sleeping within them.

target: “You don’t mean to say that your books cost no more than that! Why, there are dollars’ worth of them.” “Yes, I know there are. I had six years more of my apprenticeship to serve when you advised me ‘to be a man.’ I put by the money, which, at five cents a day, amounted to $18.25 a year, or $109.50 in six years. I keep those books by themselves, as a result of my apprenticeship cigar money; and, if you’d done as I did, you would by this time have saved many, many more dollars than I have, and would have been better off in every way and own a library besides.”

target: The "pig club" idea is one that the Negroes of the South should embrace. At this time all farmers of the South are discussing the problem which an enforced reduction of the cotton area has precipitated, and it is generally agreed that hogs and the feed crops necessary to maintain them will be the most profitable substitute for cotton. There is no doubt that the white farmers will as far as possible pay more attention to hogs hereafter than they ever have before, and the Negro farmers are just as much interested in the hog question as others.

target: Peter trusted his own heart. A man is a fool who will trust such a deceitful member, Jer. 17:9, Prov. 28:26. Peter's loud profession is answered by a definite prophecy of his utter failure, v. 30, 'Before the cock shall crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice.' Again Peter contradicts the Lord, 'I will not deny thee.' Peter's later denial of the person of Jesus in the judgment hall is prefaced by a denial of his master's assertions on the way thither. He had to learn wisdom and humility in the bitter school of experience. Peter is quite like us all, but he did learn and profited thereby (I Peter 5:5), which cannot be said of all.

target: II. Following Afar Off, vv. 53, 54. Peter “followed afar off” to the court of the high priest’s palace. We have suggested (Lesson of Nov. 1st) that zeal and affection prompted Peter, yet he was expressly forbidden and forewarned, John 13:36,38. Some one has said that the development of Peter’s weakness began in the garden when he ceased to pray. That courtyard and its brazier of coals was a dangerous place for any disciple of the Lord. The servants and soldiers of the powers against Christ were congregated about that first. It is never safe to warm oneself at the enemies’ fire though we see it constantly being done, Matt. 6:13; Ps. 1:1.

target: When Emerson advised everybody to hitch his wagon to a star, he thought the advice was impracticable, so far as the stars were concerned, for only three or four thousand stars visible, which number was far short of the demand. But when maps of the skies were made with the assistance of the telescope and camera, it was found there were 35,000,000 stars, yet quite enough for all. These stars were below the seventeenth magnitude, which was as far as the optical instruments would reach, but since then, with the aid of the new photographic plates, many more stars have been brought to view, and now the number of stars is estimated at 1,600,000,000. So there will need be no trouble about it. There is a star for every wagon, and a few left for unborn generations. Hitch your wagon to a star, and don't delay. The times demand it.—Ohio State Journal.

target: Mrs. C. J. Walker, one of the most prosperous colored business citizens of Indianapolis and one of the most successful colored women of America, is founding an industrial school in West Pondoland, South Africa. The school is modeled after the Tuskegee Institute, and has the indorsement of Booker T. Washington. She is also maintaining at Tuskegee Institute scholarships, three boys and two girls, with the hope that they will go to Africa to engage in educational work. She was left an orphan at the age of seven, and has won her own way in the world. She is the only colored woman in the world to give $1,000 to Y. M. C. A. work.

target: III. "I Know Not This Man," vs. 68-72. While at the fire, a serving maid, looking at Peter, said, "Thou also wast with Jesus of Nazareth." Peter denied it immediately and without any seeming hesitancy. Peter denied, and declared his ignorance both of the man and of the charge. He did not sympathize with what was being done to Jesus, but at heart he was not brave enough to separate himself from the multitude by whom he would not draw upon himself some sort of censure or condemnation. Thus conforming to the world about him, he made a note of denial. Having done so, he passed on to the porch and heard the first crowing of the cock. The other writers over the question of the number of maids, seems to be solved by John 18:25, where Peter is made to speak a second time. Doubtless the words of the first maid are reinforced by those of another on the same occasion, which brought the accusation against him.

They indicate the degree of his culture, his good taste and refinement or his coarseness and vulgarity. The books we collect are confessions of what we like and of what we are.

target: The most interesting of Australian types and the backbone of the dual monarchy, is the "bauer." In social rank he occupies somewhat the same position as the old English yeoman, farming his own land, and in many cases enjoying a far more substantial fortune than the nobility. The "bauer" has a strict social code of his own, mixing neither with the laborers on one hand nor the aristocracy on the other, is apparently quite content with his lot, and takes pride in his ability to provide almost all the necessities of life from the productions of his own land, even, in many cases, growing the flax from which his womenfolk weave all the household clothing.

target: Not far away, at the foot of the Canongate, is the White Horse close, and the ancient hostelry with its high steps and pointed gable where many of Prince Charlie's officers put up before the battle of Prestonpans. The Canongate itself, with its old booth and ancient houses, is crowded with the great characters of Scottish history, and the very stones played on one of the scenes that were played on that small space. The Scottish noblemen and gentlemen came through their gateways at night, came with their swords and bucklers and dirks. Many a man felt the prick of a "bare bodkin" at his throat "twixt "my Lord Seaton's house" and Playhouse close. The torches flared about the coaches and carts, and many a tall tree grew up in the night to bear human fruit in the Tolbooth to bear human fruit in the morning.

target: "Why the Rich Steal from the Shops", reads a headline in an Eastern paper. Some few cannot help it owing to a curious condition of the mind, but the general answer might be—to get something for nothing. Dr. Dubuisson, a famous brain physician of France, has given that disease, heretofore known as kleptomania, the new fangled appellation of magasinitis. This term, however, will hardly be broad enough to reach the lower classes, and taking things not your own with them will remain just plain stealing.

target: That the Anglo-Saxons of the North may realize the kind of brethren they have in the South, who like Vardaman, preach the inability of the Negro of becoming a useful citizen of this country, we reproduce an article clipped from the Congressional Record, which was read by the clerk of the House at the instigation of Mr. Crumpacker. There is no record of savages in any age being as brutal or heartless. You of the North can hardly believe that your Southern brethren would be guilty of such, but they are. Yes, they are not only guilty of this but numerous acts equally as fiendish. They are guilty also of promiscuously raping colored women and girls and of boasting of it as though they had done the commands of their God. target: The red Indians, whom we dispose of in this country, in all the bloody history of cruelty and torture, were never guilty of more savage cruelty than this. The Negroes, in their most bestial state of voodooism, could be guilty of nothing more savage and brutal. The white man is given to much boasting, but in many instances he is but a thinly veneered savage who shows his capacity and his disposition to descend rapidly to the depths. But for the restraining influence of organized society and the law this class of whites would soon descend to savagery. The conduct of mobs shows this. The more frequent the mob the more savage becomes its conduct, the more savage its excesses, the nearer the general approach to anarchy and barbarism.

target: The railway officials have been complaining for years on account of the traveling public appropriating the paraphernalia of the cars, but, when the three hundred members of the Filipino tribes were en route to St. Louis, the proverbial exception confronted them. The semi-civilized natives not only refrained from appropriating, but when the car became well heated, began to divest themselves of their own raiment, which they threw from the car windows. This continued until on some there remained articles of clothing—not one!

target: It would seem that even men of dull and muddy minds, men who have no high thoughts or worthy conceptions, who read little and think less, could see this and appreciate the fact that in observance of the law lies safety for individuals and for society. The mob feeds upon its own brutal instincts. Yesterday it sought only to hang Negro ravishers of white women, and organized society, moved by intensity of sentiment rather than guided by clear, cold judgment, rather winked at it. Today the mob mutilates, dismembers, hacks to pieces, bores into their bodies with corkscrews, and burns the bodies of Negroes for offenses which, in some cases do not amount to a misdemeanor. In this instance the Negro man had killed a white man in a fight, and the Negro woman had done nothing except to stand or to flee with her husband. It is high time for thoughtful, decent people to ask: Where is this thing to end?

target: "The Iroquois" is the name of a new publication issued by C. A. Hughes, who is located in St. Paul, Minn. Mr. Hughes was formerly a resident of this state, and gained quite a bit of notoriety while doing business in Spokane. In his salutatory he announces that, it is his debut in the field of journalism. After one has read the first page and turned to the second, and there finds the editor's picture and biographical sketch, there is no doubt of the truthfulness of his statement. "The Iroquois" will be Democratic in politics.

edto suffer the most fiendish tortures. The
blacks were foreed to hold out their hands
while one finger at a time was chopped off
The fingers were distributed as souvenirs.
The ears of the murders were cut off. Hol-
bert was severely beaten, his skull was frac-
tured, and one of his eyes, knocked out with
a stick, hung by a shred from the socket
Neither the man nor woman begged for
merey nor made a groan or plea. When the
executioners came forward to lop off fingers
Hotbert extended his hand without being
asked. The most excruciating form of pun-
ishment consisted in the use of a large cork-
serew in the hands of one of the mob. The

target: When the two Negroes were captured they were tied to trees, and while the funeral pyres were being prepared they were forced to suffer the most fiendish tortures. The blacks were forced to hold out their hands while one finger at a time was chopped off. The fingers were distributed as souvenirs. The ears of the murders were cut off. Holbert was severely beaten, his skull was fractured, and one of his eyes, knocked out with a stick, hung by a shred from the socket. Neither the man nor woman begged for mercy nor made a groan or plea. When the executioners came forward to lop off fingers, Holbert extended his hand without being asked. The most excruciating form of punishment consisted in the use of a large corkscrew in the hands of one of the mob. The instrument was bored into the flesh of the man and woman in the arms, legs and body and then pulled out, the spirals tearing out big pieces of raw, quivering flesh every time it was withdrawn.

To a large degree the young men are self governing. The discipline tends to teach the boys self control, self respect, and a regard for the wishes and rights of others. In this connection the athletic department and the Y. M. C. A. have a salient influence. The boys from this institution have done well in athletics. The special aim in these departments, wherein the boys themselves have practical control is to inculcate a sense of honor, a regard for truthfulness and honesty and an appreciation of responsibility and duty.

target: Prairie View, Texas, Feb. 20—Whatever one might say of the disposition of the South towards Negro education, it must be admitted that in recent years sentiment has gradually changed in favor of giving to the children of the Negro race a better chance to improve their condition. Practically every southern state makes some provision for the education of Negroes beyond that afforded in the public schools. Tennessee is the latest state to make such provision, the last legislature providing handsomely for the commencement of the Negro State Normal School at Nashville.

target: Montgomery, Ala., Feb. 22—Five counties in Alabama now have Negro Demonstration Agents to carry on the work of demonstration plots. There are at present Negro Demonstration Agents in nearly all the Southern states, with the exception of Virginia, which has seven agents working in eleven counties. There is no other state where so much work is being done by Negro agents for Negro farmers as in Alabama. The reason so much is being done for the Negro farmers in Alabama is undoubtedly due to the influence of Tuskegee Institute, just as the reason that so much is being done for Negro farmers in Virginia is due to Hampton Institute, of which the Tuskegee school is an offshoot.

correct: St. Albans, Feb. 21.-—Rev. J.W.
Robinsen, pastor of St. Paul Baptist
church, and teacher of the city school
has been elected superintendent of
missions in South 'Africa by the For-
eign Mission Board of the National
Baptist Convention, to have immediate
charge of the station at Cape Town
where the board owns considerable
property, including a modera brick
house for the missionaries to live in.
This is the best station that the board

decided not to accept at the present
time. His church and people are un-
willing to give him up.
target: St. Albans, Feb. 21.—Rev. J. W. Robinson, pastor of St. Paul Baptist church, and teacher of the city school has been elected superintendent of missions in South Africa by the Foreign Mission Board of the National Baptist Convention, to have immediate charge of the station at Cape Town where the board owns considerable property, including a modern brick house for the missionaries to live in. This is the best station that the board has to offer in Africa. Rev. Robinson has had the matter under consideration for some time but he has about decided not to accept at the present time. His church and people are unwilling to give him up.

target: In Texas, where more than in any other state, the Negroes have taken advantage of the opportunities offered in this new country, and have begun to make records for themselves, the state has been very liberal. In his recent trip thru Texas, Dr. Booker T. Washington repeatedly called attention to the fact in the matter of providing for the education of Negroes, Texas led all other Southern states, and that there were more Negro high schools in Texas than in all other Southern states combined. As is frequently stated by Dr. Washington, it is left largely to the educated Negro himself just to what extent the state will provide for Negro education, for more and more, it is conceded that the Negro must convince the public that education for his children is worth while.

target: A most surprising Australian bird is the kookooburra, or laughing jackass. All at once in the quiet bush come loud peals of uproarious, mocking laughter. One is not inclined to join in the merriment—it all seems as foolish and weird as if an idiot boy were disturbing a congregation in church. When the source of the laughter is located it turns out to be a silly looking bird, with clumsy, square body and open mouth, sitting unconcernedly on a stump. — National Geographic Magazine.

target: The New Era Institute at Spring Hill Baptist Church, near Hope Mills, held by Rev. Vincent last week, was one of material benefit to those ministers and brethren who attended. The attendance was not so large, but the interest manifested in the meeting was very great. Able lectures were delivered by General Missionary J. A. Whitted, District Missionary A. B. Vincent and Dr. A. W. Pegues. These lectures were of the spirit, and the lectures delivered were brimful of sound, practical Bible truths. target: Latta University will be closed during the Christmas holidays for all the season for the purpose of rebuilding the buildings that were consumed by fire last May, and also additional buildings. The University is located in the village of Oberlin, N. C., about five miles from Raleigh, the capitol building in the city. The location is the very best for a school, being out of the busy city, but within easy reach by means of the electric street cars. It is enough to say that there has not been a single case of serious illness since the establishment of the school. Each dormitory is heated by a hot water heater, so every necessary comfort is secured. The terms are very reasonable—$7.50 per month. Those desiring to reduce their expenses by work will be taken at the lowest possible rates: young men $8.40 per month — young women $.40 per month — day students $.20 per month. A small incidental fee will be charged.

target: WELDON, June 8th, 1897. Editor Gazette: On Wednesday evening, the 2d inst., the residence of Mr. John H. Howard was ablaze with light and beauty. The occasion was the marriage of his niece, Miss E. E. Chiswell, to Mr. Chas. G. Williams, of Washington, D. C. The rooms were beautifully decorated with ferns and flowers and the whole scene was one of perfect loveliness. The bride's attendants were Miss Della Story, of Wilmington, N. C.; Miss Lovie Haywood, of Raleigh, N. C.; Miss Cora Jackson, of Philadelphia, Pa. Those of the groom were Dr. Knapp, of Washington, D. C., as best man; Mr. John H. Howard, Jr., Mr. Paul Malone and Mr. Charles Hamner. The presents were both numerous and costly. After the ceremony the couple took the early morning train for a northern tour, with the best wishes of many friends. I learn their future home will be in Washington, D. C. E. J. P.

target: The National Federation of Colored Women's Clubs has come and gone, and all of Oakland did everything to make their stay pleasant and extended them every courtesy. The city council voted $500 for their entertainment, but it was up to our chamber of commerce to deny them assistance or show any courtesies. They were appealed to last September and asked the committee to come again in June. They did only to be informed that the secretary of the C. of C., who told them to call in June, was not there any more, and they knew nothing about it. I think Dr. O. R. Busch, Jones, and Dr. W. W. Purnell are members of the chamber of commerce, and pay $25 per year. I wonder for what, and will they continue?

target: city. The city offers to furnish a hand engine, and the necessary hose in good condition, together with an engine room, if the colored people will accept and organize. Our city has been remarkably lucky, not having been visited by the fire fiend for years, and then it was only a small house that burned. Notwithstanding the dry season, "roasting cars," fully developed, have been selling in our market for the past ten days. A letter written by a single woman to a married man was picked up on our streets last night by the wife of the man, before it reached its destination, and is causing a little comment.

target: The colored citizens of Travis County met in the hall of the House of Representatives on May 23, for the purpose of taking preliminary steps to celebrate the nineteenth anniversary of their emancipation; the meeting was called to order by W. S. Buckner. H. Hawkins was elected chairman and L. M. Mitchell secretary. Mr. R. B. Phillip was joined in matrimony to Miss Moore on May 18, Rev A. Grant officiating. A concert will be given by the Rosette Pleasure Club, 7, for the benefit of the M. E. Church. Elder Stater's excursion on May 12, to Brueggerhoff Station, twenty miles out on the narrow gauge railroad for the benefit of the A. M. E. Church, was largely attended and a handsome sum was realized. W. Mitchell of San Antonio, paid us a flying visit last week. Crops are suffering very much in this part of the country for want of rain. BURNETT.

target: BOSTON, May 30.—The Charles-street A. M. E. Church, Rev J. T. Jenifer pastor, celebrated its forty-third anniversary recently. There was a large attendance, the congregation containing many members of the Caucasian race, who have taken much interest in the welfare of the society. The pastor, after reading a portion of I. Corinthians, briefly sketched the history of the church from its organization in 1899, with 35 members, in a small building on Anderson street. He gave the following figures relating to the finances during his two years of service: Receipts from all sources, $27,646 84, including a conditional gift of $7,000; disbursements, for interest, $3,512 39; floating debt, $3,515 63; paid on $32,000 mortgage, $10,650.

target: BALTIMORE, May 28.—Thursday last had been set apart by the officers and managers of the Shelter for Aged and Infirm Colored People as donation day, and our many philanthropic citizens of all classes availed themselves of the opportunity and contributed very liberally in groceries, provisions and money, to fill the larders of that commendable institution. A recent annual report of the board of directors shows it to be in a healthy and vigorous condition, but greatly pressed for more room, for which purpose the late Samuel G. Wyman of Baltimore, before his decease, offered $5,000, upon the condition that a like amount be raised from other sources and strenuous efforts are being made to raise that much. There are twelve inmates in the Shelter at present and they have many wants to make them comfortable.

target: This institution is an association of lady physicians who have made the diseases of women a study for years. In our extensive practice we have been very successful in curing the many diseases so common to our sex, and, without doubt, have the greatest number of lady patients of any institution in the world. Our practice is confined exclusively to the diseases of women, and extends into every State in the Union. We are not formed entirely as a business institution, but principally for the purpose of giving medical advice and treatment to women, who, from delicacy, are often deprived of the benefit of being obliged to tell a physician of the opposite sex ALL their troubles. From our extensive experience with these diseases, we know that we understand them better than any other physician possibly can. Being both wives and mothers, we know from actual experience the

target: Decoration Day was very generally observed throughout the city. In the forenoon the different posts of the city visited the cemeteries in the outlying districts and decorated them. Post 134 visited Bainsford Island and decorated the graves at that place. Hon Julius C. Chapelle delivered the eulogy, after which the command embarked for Boston and participated in the grand parade of the Suffolk County Posts. Company L, sixth Regiment, did escort duty. The companies in the city suburbs were out in full dress uniforms and acted as escorts to the city government. The Boston Light Infantry Veteran Association appeared in their new and elegant uniforms.

The troupe of so-called Georgia 'singers' attracted much attention, their oddities being such as to cause many of those who had turned out expressly to see the show. Mr John Dickerson left this city Monday for Butterwood Beach, a popular Rhode Island summer resort, where he has been re-engaged for the season as chief cook. Mr Williams' remarks were eloquent. He was right royally entertained at the residences of prominent white citizens of the town. An interesting meeting was held at the rooms of the Garrison Lyceum last Friday evening. The program consisted of an original poem by F. J. Smith, Jr., fine music; discussion, A. H. Scales and J. B. Goddard. Remarks were made by B. R. Wilson and others. The public meetings of the Lyceum occur every two weeks and should receive the patronage of all. Hon. Geo. L. Ruffin and Capt. C. L. Mitchell have lately been added to the Honorary Board.

target: The picnic season will soon be here; the picnic is the stronghold of the average colored citizen of Nashville, and “big excursions” are a matter of course. The literary societies of Fisk University are to publish a college paper to be called The Fisk Herald. It is to be a monthly, the first number appearing in June, after which no other will be issued until the beginning of the next school year, in September. A correspondent has been appointed business manager, being also an associate editor. Having myself to give up work for THE GLOBE in this city for the Summer, friends and patrons will find the popular Mr Wm A. Crosthwaite ready to take and renew subscriptions. (ChocTaw.)

Morris displayed excellent talent in his comic parts.

target: Colored women are not generally solicited to aid in the political reforms of the day, for the reason that their white sisters do not consider their fathers, mothers' brothers, husbands or sons capable of wielding the ballot not sharing in the financial grist of the country. "Is this wrong?" Why not all lay aside their prejudice and purchase Doctress Crumpler's New Book, which advises so plainly, among other things, the prevention of Polara Infantum and many avoidable complaints that underlie the Constitution in infancy or cut short the bloom of man or womanhood. Bound in cloth, 150 pages, Price $1. P. O. Box 85, Readville, Mass. For sale by the Doctress and agents.

target: RED BANK, May 28.—Mr. Charles Cornelius was severely kicked by a horse last week. Rev J. T. Rex lectured at the benefit of the Band in Easton. The ladies of the A. M. E. Church gave a Pick-Nick Festival last week, and met with great success. Mrs. Samuel Rish, and daughters of Eatontown are stopping at Anthony Park. Mrs. David Hampton returned last week to visit a sister in Brooklyn. Prof. Malchow will take part in the concert to be given by Miller's Band. Fisk Chapel Sunday-school of Fair Haven will hold their Quarterly Meeting next Sunday. Rev. Rex, pastor of the same church, delivered an excellent sermon last Sunday from Rev vii., 7, 9, on the "Whole Church of Heaven." Gen Fisk was present at the concert of the Fisk Jubilee Singers last Friday.

target: Departed this life on Saturday morning, May 24, after a long and lingering illness, the beloved wife of Mr Joseph Lewis, at the age of twenty-four years. She was buried at St Mary's Cemetery, on Monday, May 26. The funeral cortege was very large. Rev. Eutaw Lewis officiated. The ladies of St John's Episcopal Church will open their fair on the afternoons and evenings of 6th and 7th of June for the children. The first anniversary of the Sunday-school of the Union American M. E. Church of Stapleton took place at the church on Sunday evening, May 25. Addresses were delivered by Prof J. Hewitt and singing by the children. The altar was beautified with flowers and the audience was large; the exercises were appreciated and much credit is due to the officers of the Sabbath-school. CON.

target: Our city has been filled with strangers during the past week, among whom were many Bostonians who were here on an excursion, and seemed to have enjoyed themselves seeing old friends, and viewing the many historic scenes of Richmond upon the James. May queens in all their glittering splendor copiously abound in every section of our city and among all classes. Atired in robes, decked with stars and crowns, armed with flags and streamers, they move around the May pole in such royal pageantry that retrace our thoughts to Chaucer's Fairy Queens. REUB.

target: CORPUS CHRISTI, May 24.—The time table of the Texas Mexican Railroad which runs to our city has been changed of late, and it causes a great deal of unfavorable comment and dissatisfaction from the public. By the change, our mails are delayed twelve hours at Laredo, Tex., where it misses connection with the International trains, both going and coming. By the old schedule, THE GLOBE used to reach our city every Wednesday night, while under the new change it will not arrive until Thursday.

target:
Our Baptist friends are congratulating themselves upon securing sufficient funds with which to make the first payment for their university. We rejoiced with them. They deserve substantial encouragement.

The Fourth Quarterly meeting was held at Simpson Chapel on the 15th. The reports to the Quarterly Conference show nearly $9000.00 collected for all purposes, 93 additions to the church and every department growing.

Rev. W. W. Locke rendered most acceptable service during the revival at Simpson Chapel and ranked himself among the best gospel preachers of the race. He returns home with our prayers and best wishes.

as was noted in the Recorder. What
the Recorder sets forth may be some
truth, but told tn a hard way. Mr.

target: The Freeman has no disposition nor right to call the Recorder to account for its attack on Clint Hare, candidate for county clerk, in its last week's issue. No one desires to muzzle the press; free speech is a principle of our government, etc. But Will Davis' candidacy has not been helped by quite so much freespeech as was noted in the Recorder. What the Recorder sets forth may be some truth, but told in a hard way. Mr. Hare went out to win no matter who opposed him, white men or black men; they were all Republicans. If we oppose every man that uses the word "darkey," "Negro" or even "nigger," admitting that he used the expression, there would be no one left to tell the tale. In the words of our distinguished Louisville friend, Dr. Frank, "tote squar."

target: Mr. RICHARD McGREGOR, JR., an agent of this most excellent paper, devoted, as its name purports, to the interests of the farmer and farm, is in our city, with the view of enlarging its circulation in this vicinity. We trust that he will succeed, in the object of his mission here, and in other sections of the South, for we know of no paper, devoted to similar objects, published at any point in the United States, more deserving of patronage than the one to which we are now referring. Unlike its rival, the New York "Spirit of the Times," it entirely ignores politics and party, and studiously avoids the soiling of its columns with anathemas of the South, or with the obscene and vulgar literature, so frequently abounding in that radical "Spirit."

target: Paris, February 15.—The Emperor Napoleon, in a speech, before the Corps Legislatif, on the assembling of that body, said: "Serious events have happened in Europe, almost fulfilling the great Napoleon's idea to unite all the great homogeneous nations, hitherto separated, and which is the only possible balance of power in Europe. German and Italian events have paved the way to it. Their success can not disturb France. France has been, and will be, neutral. We have not disturbed Austria. France was just and neutral. In another part of the globe we have been obliged to employ force to redress legitimate grievances, and we have endeavored to raise an ancient empire. The happy results at first obtained were compromised by an auspicious concurrence of circumstances.

target: The Senate was engaged on the District bills until the message from the House announcing the passage of Mr. Sherman's bill, with amendments, was sent in. Mr. Williams moved that the Senate concur. Several Senators regretted that the disfranchising clause was inserted by the House, but would vote to concur, notwithstanding. Mr. Johnson said that if he had his wish he would immediately receive the Southern representatives, but would vote to concur to the majority. He would vote for the bill because he saw in it a mode of rescuing the country from perils that now threaten it.

target: France will use her new rights wisely. She is respected abroad, but the conditions of war being changed, she must increase her defenses.—The army bill has been so drawn as to lighten the burden in times of peace and increase the resources in times of war. Our neighbors assume far heavier burdens. Let us keep the flag high as the best means of preserving peace. Our prosperity—France advances. The indirect receipts have increased fifty millions of francs, and we shall soon be able to satisfy all interests and reduce the land tax whereby the working classes are to be benefited and liberty to be solid, lasting and glorious. I trust in my policy, my right and my conscience.”

target: Second, I have no faith in conventions. They are generally controlled by the least wise among their body. They are excitable mobs, and in every convention there are a few men who are wiser than all the rest, but these men are controlled, and do not control. The course of the Democratic Convention in 1864, in my judgment, lost McClellan his election. The Convention last summer at Philadelphia did no good at all, to say the least. And the dangers of conventions are increased in times so excitable and electrical as ours.

target: More differences of opinion representing measures of public policy, or as to the means of accomplishing the same ends, should be no cause of ill-feeling or personal animosity between gentlemen of the same or between gentlemen of different political profession. It shall, therefore, be our constant aim in the future, as in the past, to treat with proper consideration and courtesy the adverse opinions of others; to cultivate and maintain terms of amicable relationship with our contemporaries, and to endeavor to reconcile all mere personal feuds and differences. Nevertheless, we shall compromise no principle for the sake of harmony, acquiesce in no popular fallacy nor, for sake of unanimity, bow to the behests of controlling prejudice for the sake of avoiding the less, nor, when controversy, shall be non-contentious, open and candid for being considerate. target: Associated with Col. Boyd, the principal manager, is a gentleman of large experience in the management and conduct of lotteries. We allude to Mr. L. R. BROADBENT, of Baltimore, who is known as "Agent" for the Lottery. This gentleman is, in the estimation of the people of Maryland, and we are assured by an eminent citizen of Georgia who has had business transactions with him for many years, deservedly so. By the Legislature of his own State, he was constituted the Chief Manager, or Inspector of the drawings of the lotteries there, in order to prevent frauds, a trust of which he acquitted himself to the entire satisfaction of the people of that State. Securing the services of so honorable and so experienced an individual, the corporators of the "Georgia State Lottery" have displayed much wisdom.

target: We have been thus prolix on the subject of this "Lottery," for reasons before stated. Considering the benevolent nature and objects of its creation, we could do less. We are at the standpoint of its principal office and where the drawings will take place. We shall have personal knowledge of each one of the "Board of Managers," who, the public are assured by the Principal Manager, will be "some of the best citizens of the State," and while we have now every confidence in the integrity of every one of the parties connected with its present management, our readers may be assured that we shall faithfully report any violation of the provisions of the grant, should any such occur.

target: The Origin of Sherman's Substitute. — A Washington date of the 17th gives the following as the origin of Sherman's substitute, adopted in the Senate, and subsequently rejected in the House: — In the Senate at the close of Saturday night's session, Mr. Blaine's amendment pending, the Republican Senators held a caucus at 11 o'clock Saturday morning to agree about a common basis, if possible. At this caucus the whole subject was referred to a committee of Senators, viz: Messrs. Fessenden, Trumbull, Sumner, Howard, Sherman, Harris and Frelinghuysen. The committee was at work the most of Saturday, and finally agreed upon the bill which Senator Sherman offered upon the statute at the Saturday evening session, and which was adopted, after a whole night's discussion. — The last section of the substitute is simply Mr. Blaine's amendment, somewhat changed in phraseology, but covering just the same points.

target: The franchise bill passed its third reading yesterday afternoon in the Senate, and the colored suffrage amendment, without qualification of property or education, is now an article of our State Constitution. This gratifying victory was won by the decisive vote of 14 to 7. It has placed Tennessee, after a two years' struggle, kept up without an hour's intermission, in the van of the army of freedom. The bill had its faults, we believed, that we would gladly have removed, but its passage enfranchised some 50,000 colored men, and that was enough to conceal all its defects, when we reflected that its defeat would have doomed the State to the bloody rule of Andy Johnson's partisans.

target: RATHER PREMATURE.—The following appears in the Savannah Republican as a special dispatch: WASHINGTON, D. C., Feb. 17, 1867.—Georgia, six years ago the great and glorious 'Empire State of the South' is to-day a Province, the vote declaring this painful fact, having, in the Senate Chamber to-day, received twenty-seven ayes to seven nays. You will receive further particulars of this most important act through the Associated Press, and from H. A. G. Lee. No you don't. The Empire State of the South is not, at this writing four days after the above date, a mere Province. It still enjoys a few, if not all, of the immunities and prerogatives of a State. But they 'like to have did it.' Yet a 'miss is as good as a mile.' It is astounding to what haste, zeal and desire sometimes hurry people.

target: The enfranchisement of near-fifty thousand loyal freemen is a deed of no ordinary magnitude and lustre. When we look back upon the darkness and terror of 1861, under secession Democratic rule and the reign of terror, to the almost cloudless sunrise of Republicanism in 1867, the advance seems beyond all the ordinary rules of political progress, and brings back the days of miracles when the Almighty visibly interposed for the good of his people, and the earth acknowledged the presence and animation of the descending Deity. It was a grand, glorious day's work. It has clothed the present State Government with imperishable splendor. In behalf of the loyal white voters of the State, of the loyal, well-enfranchised colored voters, who yesterday put on the sacred garments of citizenship, we say with overflowing hearts, All hail the Tennessee Senate!

target: Fenian Excitement in New York. The news of an uprising in Ireland has had the effect to set the Fenian circles throughout the country to work again, with renewed energy. A mass meeting has been called in New York city, and money is being contributed in a very liberal way, and in individual instances in considerable sums. Col. Roberts states that if any practical movement of a formidable warlike character has begun in Ireland, the Brotherhood of which he is head will furnish all the aid in their power until successful. In the meantime the circles, under instructions of Col. Roberts, are preparing for another campaign against Canada, which promises to be of formidable character.

target: By the breaking away of the banks of the Des Paines River, yesterday, a large portion of Southwestern Chicago was inundated, the habitations of at least 10,000 persons, being surrounded by water. Quick ascents were made to higher regions, and rafts were constructed, on which the beleaguered people embarked from upper windows. During the evening the flood somewhat subsided, but broke out afresh to-day, covering a still wider area of territory. The water in the inundated regions varies in depth from a few inches to three or four feet. The loss is very heavy, but can not now be estimated.

target: MOVEMENTS OF COTTON.—The Savannah Republican states that the movement of cotton is becoming large, not only in the number of bales, but in value. The receipts of cotton at the ports for the four weeks ending February 2, were about three hundred thousand bales, and the exports to foreign ports during the same time about one hundred and eighty thousand bales. The gold value of the exports was about eighteen million dollars. This large sum, in connection with the value of exports of tobacco, provisions and other products, will account for the depression in exchange and the partial failure of the efforts to advance the premium on gold. It is not improbable that the exports of cotton will average, till early summer, one hundred and fifty thousand bales per month.

target: Grape and Elderberry Preserve.—This is delicious. To make it allow equal quantities of elderberries and grapes and use a grape with decided, rich, full flavor, for the sweetness of the elderberries makes up for much tartness of grape. The grapes must be opened with a very sharp knife and the seeds must be removed. Allow as much sugar as the elderberries and grapes together weigh. Put the fruit in a preserving kettle and barely cover with cold water. Bring to the boiling point, skim and add a fourth of the sugar. Bring to the boiling point again, add another quarter of the sugar, boil for 10 minutes, repeat until the sugar is all used and then boil until a little of the sirup jellies on a plate on the ice.

target: WASHINGTON.—The result of the government's experimental work in horse breeding, begun in 1913, is interesting. Unlike foreign governments, the United States had made no experiments in the selection of horses for army uses, and it was only as the result of a recommendation to congress by a number of experts that had inspected foreign studs that an appropriation of $50,000 was set aside and the work turned over to the department of agriculture, which in turn delegated it to the bureau of animal husbandry. The object was to produce desirable types of cavalry remounts and artillery horses. Thoroughbred sires had been pronounced most serviceable by continental experts and the project was undertaken along the same lines here, though there was a desire to try other light horse types of stallion, and a few trotting, saddle and Morgan sires were included in the project.

target: By morning we see them move on to the public square before Pilate's palace. The Jews are not to be permitted to enter the Judgment Hall lest they be thereby defiled and unable to eat the passover which was then being kept. Therefore we see Jesus entering alone with Pilate. During the short interview Pilate asks: "Art thou the king of the Jews?" Jesus answers: "My kingdom is not of this world." Pilate reappears. The square is crowded and the clamor is great. Pilate asks: "Shall I release to you the king of the Jews?" But the clamoring reply is: "Not this man, but Barabbas." Pilate now orders that Jesus be scourged. He is then possibly running to a pile of thorn bushes such as are still extensively gathered and kept for fuel, plaited into a crown, and placed upon his head, also a purple robe. Thus he is presented to his adversaries outside, and Pilate's voice is heard above the din: "Ecce Homo" (Behold the man).

target: Thus in our mind's journey we have followed closely St. John's graphic account given in the eighteenth and nineteenth chapters. The thirteenth verse of the latter chapter is of all important interest to our subject. 'Pilate therefore . . . brought Jesus forth, and sat down in the judgment seat in a place called pavement.' The words here translated in the authorized version 'judgment seat' have been rendered in a more critical translation by J. B. Rotherham 'elevated bench.' In the original Greek it is 'bema,' meaning an elevated platform for orators' use. Nor does the context go to show that it was anything like an official throne or judgment seat.

target: Here on one side of the street we find the Roman castle, on the other a large paved court taking the place of the ancient forum, or place of justice, and home of the governor should be situated by this city square seems quite natural, and the Bible accounts go far to make it clear. Having thus fixed the surroundings in our minds, we stand off in amazement, for we see almost small squads of Roman soldiers, while off duty from the castle, lounging here or passing the time with their games which they themselves have marked out on the pavement, or it may be we shall soon see a large crowd congregating here, listening to some orator standing above the audience on one of these stone benches, or "bema," as they were called, and placed there for the purpose.

target: from the sources of their supplies. Mr Sherman persuaded congress to appropriate $12,000 for steel shelters along Big B and Little B streets, N. W., and then called on congress to appropriate $50,000 for permanent shelters and wharves on the Potomac valley. Mr. Sherman's enthusiasm in his attempt to reduce the living expenses of the masses in the city of Washington is not to be confined to the District or as a working model for other cities whose infant death rate and racial suicide are statistically known to increase proportionately with the cost of foodstuffs. Washington feels that there is a splendid purpose back of this experiment, far-reaching in its various individual phases, which extend from the hard-working farmer to the poor market woman, whose household expenses are such that there is no chance to lay aside enough for the education of her children.

target: Jerusalem has been besieged and destroyed more times than any other city. Nearly 2,000 years have elapsed since the days we have been thinking of. Naturally we cannot, nor should we expect, to find here absolutely certain spots associated with our Lord's life on earth. In fact few of the many now pointed out are capable of being authenticated. Nevertheless, as we turn away from this spot we cannot help but feel we have looked upon the very “pavement” trod by our Lord, and probably have seen the very seat from which Pilate gave sentence.

The appearance was a very brilliant silvery flash, as vivid and quick as a flash of lightning. But two things are necessary to see bullets fly, a dark background and the sun at right angles to its course. Big gun projectiles are, of course, nearly always visible, and it is not hard to see a charge of shot in flight if one knows what to look for and where to stand.

target: A case of bigamy that sounds queer to western ears is reported from India. One Ahad Ali married Fuljan Bibi about eleven years ago when the latter was only 3 years old. The former lodged a complaint before the sub-divisional officer of Bagerhat alleging that one Mojam Dakua had married his lawful wife Fuljan last January. Both Mojam and Fuljan were arrested and committed to the sessions. In the sessions court both the accused were found guilty. Mojam was sentenced to one year's rigorous imprisonment and Fuljan to two months' simple imprisonment.

target: The specimen is believed to be that of a full-grown male, although not an old individual, who lived in the pleistocene, probably 150,000 years ago. This animal, while it is related to the elephant family, differs in general proportions of the skeleton and through the character of its teeth, which unlike the flat, grinding ones of the elephant, are more of a rugose type with pointed corrugations calculated to have been used in crushing as well as grinding the coarse vegetable food on which it lived. The largest of the teeth measures nearly seven inches in length and is about four inches across, weighing several pounds.

target: The advancement in the modes and rapidity of traveling has been very great. We are told that Winthrop, an early Governor of Massachusetts, having lived two years in Boston, wanted to visit Plymouth, of the same State, about fifty miles distant. After two days, we are told, he reached Plymouth, "having been conveyed over the fords of streams on the shoulders of Indians." Toward the close of the eighteenth century it was regarded as a remarkable journey if the traveler went from Edinburgh, Scotland, to London, England, in three days and three nights. It was said that several persons, who had been so rash as to attempt it, had actually died from the rapidity of the motion.

input:
correct: The era of slang is upon us with a
breadth that is almost appalling. Not
only the slang that might be defined
as the burlesque or colloquinl form of
expression, the languageof low humor,
or the jargon of thievend vagrants
but a species that isalmost as repre-
hensible. It will not do to apologize
forit by saying that “slang is probably
as old as human speech,” and that the
early weiters indulged in it. especinlly
the Greek and Boman dramatiste; and
whilewe may speak and write against
the pernicious habit. we suspect that
we will not grow disgusted enough
with it to thoroughly uproot it until it
has reached its climax. The worst fact
about it is that it is not contined to the
low and the iliterate, but has invaded
the public schools, cultured socioty.
and the literature of our books. I admit
that some of the slang expressions
are forcible and full of adequateness,
among which I might name “fired
out,” colossal cheek,” eto. Still, even
they ought to be tabooed.
target: The era of slang is upon us with a breath that is almost appalling. Not only the slang that might be defined as the burlesque or colloquial form of expression, the language of low humor, or the jargon of thieves and vagrants, but a species that is almost as reprehensible. It will not do to apologize for it by saying that "slang is probably as old as human speech," and that the early writers indulged in it, especially the Greek and Roman dramatists; and while we may speak and write against the pernicious habit, we suspect that we will not grow disgusted enough with it to thoroughly uproot it until it has reached its climax. The worst fact about it is that it is not confined to the low and the illiterate, but has invaded the public schools, cultured society, and the literature of our books. I admit that some of the slang expressions are forcible and full of adequateness, among which I might name "fired out," "colossal cheek," etc. Still, even they ought to be tabooed.

target: But what excuse can possibly be offered for such words as "galliot," "sardine," "chump," "kicker," "kid," etc? Or such expressions as "let her go, Gallagher," "Waltzed off on her ear," "I should snicker," "Now you're shoutin'," etc. They are scarcely emphatic, and certainly not polite. Even the fair sex have caught the infection and speak about "his royal nibs" or the "howling swell." The girl of to-day is ready to "bet her bottom dollar," wants to know "what you're givin' her," lets you know that you are "off your base," and insists that you shall "come off," "vanose," "skedaddle," "absquatulate," and all that. You do her a slight favor and she exclaims: "O, thanks, awfully!" Why she should thank you with "reverend fear" is beyond your comprehension. Ask her to sing your sentimental ballad and she will probably say: "O, really, Mr. ——, I cawn't. It's too utterly too-too!"

target: It has often been asked how long it would take to refill the Lake of Geneva (Lake Leman) if it were empty. The lake covers an area of 577,860,000 square meters (225 miles). Its greatest depth is 312 meters (nearly 1,024 feet), and its average depth may therefore be assumed to be 150 meters (nearly 490 feet). In accordance with this supposition the lake contains 90 milliards of cubic meters (close upon 20,000 milliards of gallons) of water. The average inflow from the Rhone per second is 27 cubic meters (5,948 gallons), or 2,332,880 cubic meters (513,425,972 gallons) in the 24 hours. To fill the basin of the lake, therefore, the Rhone would require 106 years.—N. Y. Post.

target: —An appearance as of being hol-low-ed out has been remarked in the surface of the hard, green sandstone rocks, near Lima, Peru, and was ascribed by Sir Charles Lyell to the result of water action on ancient and subsequently elevated sea beaches. Mr. Nation, of Lima, however, who has been observing the rocks for twenty-five years, is satisfied that the hollows are increasing in size and in number. He believes that they are the work of a cryptogamic plant, a lichen, which is in active vegetation during the foggy season, the swelling of whose cells causes a scaling of the rock.

target: One of the prettiest holiday parties was given Wednesday evening by the Omega Phi Psi fraternity of the University of Minnesota at Pioneer hall, 588 Rondo street. From four to six of the frat boys initiated into their midst De Vaughn Yates of Billings, Mont., a student in civil engineering. Later they were joined by their lady guests, who appeared in evening attire and were handsomely gowned. The hall was a bower of beauty in the holiday colors, confetti streamers and shaded lights. Delicious frappe was served between dances. Sidney Williams presided at the piano, and dancing was enjoyed until the wee small hours. The committee, Messrs. Donald Brown, Earl Kyle and Walter Minor, are due much credit for their untiring efforts in making this the most gala affair during the holiday season.

target: Richmond, Va.—The Union eleven defeated the crack Hampton squad here last week to the score of 7-6. Hampton was up against a far heavier team than their own. In the first three quarters of the game their defense held Union scoreless. By a series of line plunges they managed to get the ball within scoring distance, where Coleman carried it over for a touchdown. They tried for the extra point but failed. This lone touchdown was the only one scored on Union this season. During the early part of the final period Union got the ball over with drives and clinched the game with the extra point scored on the goal kick. Before the game was over Hampton had their chance to win their game but the timer's whistle stopped the game as Gunn was about to kick.

Frazier, 618 East First street, prominent physician, was fined $50 and given a 10 days' jail sentence in the police court when he was found guilty of the charge preferred by his wife that he threatened her with a deadly weapon. His wife, Mrs. Dora C. Frazier, who is of one of the first families of Kentucky, formerly taught in the city schools of Louisville, and at present is teaching at Crofton. Through her attorney she has entered suit for $10,000 against the physician along with a bill for divorce in which she charges cruelty, non-support and adultery.

them without trial and without any
recourse to law and order——."
target: “I quite agree with you on that,” answered the Jew, with a smile. “That’s just why I wanted to feel that our minds were together, before saying what I have in mind. I’m sorry you did not proclaim that belief in your state legislature. I’m sorrier still that you are not where legislation on that definition is sadly needed right now. Last year the National Congress tried, in an appropriate way, to get at some anarchists in this country—right in the state where you doubtlessly reside. They tried to make a law to prevent some anarchists in this country from lynching American citizens—from executing them without trial and without any recourse to law and order—.”

input:
correct: Vermont's recent refusal to go back
to statewide prohibition will have a
tendency to boost the local option
movement in this and other states.
Some fifty years ago Vermont went
dry and remained dry until 1903, when
the present local option law was adopt-
ed, on referendum, by a majority of
729 votes. The law has worked well,
but the prohibitionists were not satis-
fied and finally suceeeded in having
the question referred to the people
There will be no further effort to force
prohibition upon that state. And pub-
lie sentiment is the same fn other
states where local option prevails.
target: Vermont's recent refusal to go back to statewide prohibition will have a tendency to boost the local option movement in this and other states. Some fifty years ago Vermont went dry and remained dry until 1903, when the present local option law was adopted, on referendum, by a majority of 729 votes. The law has worked well, but the prohibitionists were not satisfied and finally succeeded in having the question referred to the people. There will be no further effort to force prohibition upon that state. And public sentiment is the same in other states where local option prevails.

target: In discussing the accomplishments of the army service in the consummation of the army program, it will be well to put forward enthusiastically and persistently put forward by word of mouth and in the press. We have seen and heard much of the proposed aviation program for the United States within the next year. In a country where one great industry produces 1,500,000 motors per year, the fabrication of 100,000 planes might seem a simple matter. But the experience of three years of practical experience in the war shows that there are now between 40 and 50 men of the auxiliary services required for each active machine. If this ratio were to be adhered to in our service, it would mean that some 4,000,000 men would be required in our aeronautical department on foreign soil between the lines of embarkation and the fighting front.

target: It is worthy of remark that the more recent decisions of the courts of the Northern States, so far as they have come under our observation, sustain the doctrine of a right to a stay. While the courts in general of the Southern States, where the condition of the country made the stay much more necessary to the welfare of the people than in the Northern States, have been less disposed to recognize it, the courts in Pennsylvania, the doctrine is well settled, that the Legislature may pass a stay-law, prohibiting the enforcement of contracts in existence, for a definite and reasonable time; and it has been ruled that a stay of three years is not an unreasonable period. This has been the rule of decision in that great State since the year 1831.

target: Under the old Constitution the Supreme Court was obliged to deliver its decisions in every case that came before it, at the first term, except for providential cause, and the creditor, if the judgment were in his favor, had the right to the benefit of the decision at the first term. The new Constitution authorizes the Court, in its discretion, to withhold its decision till the second term. This may delay the creditor six months in making his collection; but it will not probably be intended that the Supreme Court violates the obligation of contracts made prior to the new Constitution, by holding up its decision till the second term.

target: Chadwick vs. Moone, 8 Watts & Sergt., 49, it was held, that local statutes which suspend, for a reasonable time, execution of a judgment on a previous contract, are not prohibited by the Constitution of the United States, and that the statute passed by the Legislature of that State in 1842, suspending, for a year, a sale under execution for less than two-thirds of the appraised value, is not unconstitutional. That able jurist, Chief Justice Gibson, delivers the opinion of the court, and cites from the Illinois statute held by the Supreme Court of the United States to be unconstitutional, 2 How., 608, in this cardinal feature, that its prohibitions of execution was perpetual, while in the statute under consideration, the suspension of execution was limited. In other words, one might entirely destroy the remedy, while the other, as in the case at bar, only postponed the period of its performance.

target: In Baumbach vs. Bade, 9 Wisconsin R-ps, 559, the Supreme Court of that State fully sustains the constitutionality of a stay-law. The same doctrine is ably sustained by the Supreme Court of Iowa, in McCormick vs. Busch, see Law Reg. for December, 1863. In this case the learned Chief Justice, delivering the opinion, says: "We have found no case which holds that a law, giving the right to a stay of execution upon certain terms, would be invalid, as applied to prior contracts, unless it be certain cases in Kentucky, which seem to be based upon certain peculiar provisions in the statute."

target: The pleas filed in this case were such as were necessary to let in the evidence on the trial, and such as were proper under the section of the Act. Counsel for plaintiff demurred to the pleas on the ground that the act was unconstitutional and void. The court sustained the demurrer, and ordered the pleas to be stricken from the record. 1. It cannot be questioned that the courts have the power to declare acts of the Legislature unconstitutional, null and void; and to refuse, on that ground, to enforce them. While this is a power which should be exercised with great caution. Solemn acts of the Legislature are always presumed to be constitutional and binding, and should never be set aside by the courts, except in clear and urgent cases. If the court entertains doubts, the decision should be in favor of the validity of the act. 12 Wheat. 270, 16 Ga. R. 103.
