target: There have been various definitions of the obligation of a contract. Mr. Justice Baldwin, in the case of McCracker vs. Hayward, says: "The obligation of a contract consists in the power to enforce on one party what he has promised." Mr. Justice Trimble, in Ogden vs. Sanders, says: "The obligation of the contract consists in the power and efficacy of the law, which applies to and enforces performance of the contract, or to payment of its equivalent, or both." And, once, Mr. Webster, in the argument of the same case, defines it to be "the duty of performing a legal agreement." Whatever may be the correct definition, (and upon this point scarcely any two Judges agree,) it is positively certain that the laws of the State, in existence at the time, from part of it, is untenable.

target: The presumption of the law is, that A will keep his promise, and if he does so, the remedial laws of the State have no connection whatever with the contract. But, suppose the contract is broken by non-performance, the obligation still exists, and A is still under the duty of performing his remedy to enforce performance. But what remedy? The particular one afforded by the laws of the State in which the contract was made, or the one afforded to suitors at the time the aid of the courts is sought? Unquestionably the latter. If the former were his right, he would be entitled to it, no matter where the contract was made. If a contract were made in New York, and the suit was brought in Georgia, the plaintiff would, if that be the rule, be entitled to have the remedial laws of New York, which were in existence at the time the contract was made, applied in the courts of Georgia, which is contrary to the practice and decisions of all the courts.

target: The late Chief Justice Marshall, who was certainly one of the ablest jurists of any age, who characterizes the conduct of a State, which would refuse to afford remedies to enforce contracts, in very strong terms of reproach, admits that the State is not under any obligation to do so, and denies that there is any coercive power over her, to compel her to enforce the performance of contracts. In Ogden v. Saunders, 12 Wheat., 350-1-2, he says: 'Our country exhibits the extraordinary spectacle of distinct, and in many respects, independent governments, over the whole of which, the general and State governments are restrained from impairing the obligation of contracts, but they furnish the remedy in tribunals constituted by themselves. It has been shown that the obligation is distinct from the remedy, and it would seem to follow, that the obligation rests upon the party, without acting on the obligation. To afford a remedy is certainly the high duty of those who govern to those who are governed.'

target: The very illustrations given by the learned Judge show that he does not intend to lay down the broad proposition contended for, in this Court, that the law of the remedy existing at the time enters into the contract, and becomes part of it. Mr. Justice Swayne says: "Illustrations of the doctrine are found in the implied obligation of the debtor to pay interest after the maturity of the debt, when the contract is silent; in the liability of the drawer of a protested bill to pay exchange for damages, and in the right of the drawer and indorser to require proof of demand and notice." These illustrations show what is meant by the general proposition of the learned Justice, and are consistent with the position that the Legislature may pass laws acting upon the remedy, while it may not impair the obligation of the contract.

target: If the Legislature can exempt the body entirely from seizure for the non payment of a debt now in existence, why can it not exempt a portion of it, or a second, or any other piece of property? Can a solid legal reason be given to sustain the one, that will not apply with equal force to the other? If one impairs the obligation of the contract, the other does; both do, or neither does. If the Legislature may exempt the body, and there is no legal reason why it may not, why can it not reverse the rule, and exempt the property, and leave the body subject? Whatever may be said about the humanity of the age allowing the one and revolting at the other, the legal reason State over the remedy is the same. I have seen no successful attempt by the advocates of the rule I am now combating to draw a solid distinction between the two cases, supported by logical legal reasons. I expect to see none, for the simple reason that the legal distinction does not exist.

target: Again, the change made by the new Constitution greatly accelerates the collection of debts in this State. Under the old law, the defendant might file his pleas (generally not under oath) at the first term, and might elect to stand for trial by a jury at the second term. But at that time either party had the right to a continuance of the case upon a proper showing. After each party had a continuance, the case must be tried by the petit jury, unless the parties stipulated, or one of them appealed to a special jury. The case then stood for trial again at the next term. But each party was entitled to two continuances on the appeal, on proper showing, as matter of right, and to as many more for providential cause as the Court might allow. This might amount to a delay of several years before final judgment.

target: The case of Mason vs. Haile, decided by the Supreme Court of the United States, reported in 12 Wheat., 370, is a strong one. In that case Haile was in prison, having the benefit of prison labor, under a first process against Mason, and was discharged by resolution of the Legislature of Rhode Island, without the payment of the debt; and the Supreme Court held, that this resolution did not impair the obligation of the contract. The court says: "This is a measure which must be regulated by the views of policy and expediency entertained by the State Legislatures. Such laws act mainly upon the remedy, and not upon the contract. They do not take away the entire remedy, but only so far as to imprisonment form a part of such remedy."

target: Mr. Justice Story, in his commentaries on the Constitution, section 1331, says: "Rights may indeed exist, without any present, adequate, correspondent remedies, between private persons. Thus, a State may refuse to allow imprisonment for debt, and the debtor may have no property. But still the right of the creditor remains, and he may enforce it against the future property of the debtor." Again, in section 1335, he says: "The State Legislature may discharge a party from imprisonment upon a judgment in a civil case of contract, without infringing the Constitution, for this is but a modification of the remedy, and does not impair the obligation of the contract."

target: The same doctrine is held in the case of Burges vs. Crowninshield, 4 Wheat., 200, where Chief Justice Marshall says: 'The distinction between the obligation of a contract, and the remedy given by the Legislature to enforce that obligation, has been taken at the bar, and exists in the nature of things. Without impairing the obligation of the contract, the remedy may certainly be modified as the wisdom of the nation shall direct.' In that case, the insolvent law of New York, which discharged the defendant from imprisonment without the payment of the debt, was sustained by the court.

target: As one result from the failure of the Bank of Georgia to redeem their bills in specie, we have to note the fact, that on the Western & Atlantic Rail Road, a considerable number of persons are compelled to make settlements under a dollar. Consequently the Depot Agents and Conductors are compelled to have silver or submit to a loss. We learn that they have been forced to buy this coin at a premium of five per cent, with which to make change. As the transactions on this road will be the concern of the year amount to a very large sum, it will be at once seen, that the State of Georgia is losing five per cent. on Thousands of Dollars. The State will consequently be, probably, the heaviest loser under the present system. What is the remedy? The people of the State think of this? Five per cent sunk on a large portion of the receipts of the Western & Atlantic Rail Road, because the Banks don't pay their Bills in gold and silver.

target: When informed that a mob was coming to assail him, and that, if he desired to save himself, he must leave the house immediately, he replied: 'I have twenty balls in my pocket, and twenty lives at my command.' Mr. Walker then expostulated with him: 'I tell you on the word of a gentleman, that I will leave by one o'clock.' He was told that it would be too late then; that he must get away immediately, or that the arrival of another delegate of a different character was heard in the office below. This was the d-ird officer. Mr. Walker, on his coat, was shown the way to the back part of the house, from which, by scaling the walls of a cotton yard, he reached Lopocomb Street, and thence the river. During the night, the Mayor ordered a posse of police to remain at the Garner House to guard his protection, as he should make his escape. He thought it safer that he is still in the city. Whether or not he is the case, remains yet to be seen.

A Democratic Senator from Kentucky.

We are advised says the Washington Union that Ex-Governor L. W. Powell was elected to the U. S. Senate by the Legislature of Kentucky on Tuesday last, for six years from the 4th of March, 1859. This is, indeed, a gratifying result, and is the first fruits of the democratic victory of 1855 and 1857 in the good State of Kentucky, the home of our distinguished Vice President. Governor Powell has not been a member of either house of Congress, but as Governor of Kentucky, the sole democratic interruption to a long line of opposition governors, his name and reputation became familiar to the country.

target: Thirty-four guests sat down to the spread this year, service continuing from 10 o'clock until 12. It is needless to recount the menu, for it lived up to the name and reputation of the Denver Club, and the food was served with epicurean excellence. It was served with a brilliance of scintillating cut glass, silverware, china and napery. And the guests were garbed for such a feast. The gentlemen in the newest styles of the fashionably modish creations which Denver's modistes contrived for the ladies. And not Denver alone, for if report is accepted, Chicago and the East contributed their share of pretty apparel to grace the guests. Glorious colors, soft alluring fabrics, sparkling jewelry, and a thousand other accessories made the fashion's triumph complete.

target: Every Sunday morning there is a Bible class, and in the evening a general service. Sunday, Nov. 24, 500 present. Sunday evening, Dec. 1, there were just 520 to attend, and Sunday, Dec. 8, 557 were present. The post, consisting of the first battalion second field Artillery, companies C, D, E, F, G and H, 24th infantry, has some 1,700 men. To get one-third of these men to a religious service is the problem that chaplain Gladden seems to have solved. He has purchased electric light plant and lights the post hall and the bowling alleys with electricity. This adds much to the comfort of the men and induces them to attend. The chaplain also runs a moving picture show during the week. These pictures serve to educate as well as amuse a large number of men as always there.

target: Murray's historical and biographical encyclopedia of the colored race throughout the world in six volumes will be published and will be issued at once. For fifteen years the author, who is an assistant in the library of Congress, has been actively engaged in collecting the necessary data for this monumental work. It is a record of the achievements of the foremost men and women of the colored race from the time of Ishmael, son of Abraham, down to date; there are fully 25,000 biographical sketches which accentuate the historical volumes and a bibliography of 10,000 titles. The pamphlets representing the contributions of the race to the world's literature. And further, a list of all works of fiction and drama dealing with the race question as a feature. Price twenty-four dollars ($24.00) per set of six volumes. Readers may do business in monthly payments.

target: The religious services are usually short, consisting of a short practical talk or sermon, prayer and song service, and seem glad of the opportunity. The services are undenominational, but are strictly religious and of a persuasive nature. The chaplain is a man who is fond of the men peculiarly his own. He knows them personally and all feel that they have a friend that they can confide in, not over burdened with sentimentality, but interested in the welfare and salvation of the men, and a hearty greeting and a ready, wholesome advice and counsel to those in trouble. He gets a hold on men that seldom seen in service character.

target: Because of the $25,000 which is offered by a Chicago philanthropist to any colored man who will build a Y. M. C. A. for the colored work, considerable interest attaches to the sum of money in rails under the leadership of the local organization of which Mr. DeFrantz is secretary, and which is known as the Negro Boy Scouts. The daily press upon the need of such an organization has put some money worth $1,000 in favor of it in a manner that does credit to the interest of the people of Kansas City. It is said that the millions poured into foreign mission fields gives reason for the spending of a few hundred dollars to save a Negro boy from a vicious environment. target: Known throughout the country as the second Williams & Walker. These, King & Bailey, this most popular team, with the greatest success at the Winter Garden, Berlin, Germany. To the editor, L. E. Pryor, Denver: This is the hit of the East. It is that of Mr. Gideon's twelve Dandy Dixies Co. The act is a storm from start to finish, everything fast and snappy. The company is composed of the following well-known artists: Edith Logan, Alma Drymon, Eva Sprague, Eva Moore, Hattie May and Lizzie Baily; Billy Washington, Irwin Richardson, Ottis Benson, Joseph Boxley and Walter Robinson. These are now playing at the Palace Theatre, Providence, R. I., Dec. 5th; Palace Theatre, Boston, Boduin Theatre, Boston Scientific Theatre, Boston. We are booked until Christmas night in Boston, go to Canada after. We will write you from Montreal.

target: An aged Colored woman died at Charleston, W. V., last week, it is said, was 102 years of age. plety are a power in society for the

target: The trustees realized then as now, that institutions of learning, having the corporate force of men of intelligence and piety are a power in society for the formation of public sentiment, and that this public sentiment ought to be against vice in all its forms; for vice is disintegrating and will sooner or later destroy society. They saw clearly that slave holding was subversive of every element of justice, that caste was barbarous in nature, and withering in its effects upon the aspirations of those who were the victims of its barbarous spirit. That sectarianism was contrary to the spirit of the gospel, and the enemy of all true reformers. The design therefore was that the teaching influence institution should be positive in its influence against these great vices of society and hinderances to righteousness.

input:
correct: Tan ArraL can not in common fair-
ness, advocate the merits of this or that
preacher. Two things are of the first
importance in order to hope for success.
The first is to make selection of a
preacher who is possessed of qualities
essential to popularity. Havingselected
a candidate the second step is to work
for his success. Let one secure all the
ballote possible and at the same time sti
up interest and activity in others. De-
lay in securing ballots are as dangerous
as delays in otber mattters. It would
worry one much to learn that his or her
condidate might have been elected by
a slight extra effort. A few votes may
be the difference between the winners
and losers. Hence the importance of
early and persistent activity in securing
ballots. The plan of determining whe
the most popular minister is, is given in
order that all may thoroughly under-
stand.
target: THE APPEAL can not in common fairness, advocate the merits of this or that preacher. Two things are of the first importance in order to hope for success. The first is to make selection of a preacher who is possessed of qualities essential to popularity. Having selected a candidate the second step is to work for his success. Let one secure all the ballots possible and at the same time stir up interest and activity in others. Delay in securing ballots are as dangerous as delays in other matters. It would worry one much to learn that his or her candidate might have been elected by a slight extra effort. A few votes may be the difference between the winners and losers. Hence the importance of early and persistent activity in securing ballots. The plan of determining who the most popular minister is, is given in order that all may thoroughly understand.

input:

purchased a tract of land as a site for
our future college building and in the
latter part of the year 1869, 1 went cast-
ward to solicit funds with which to pay
for our lands. During this absence the

Boren were driven out by mob violence
The war of the Rebellion came, and our
college plans were suspended until that
war was over. Speedily after the war
our school was resumed and Colored
pupils, by design, mtroduced. Opposi-
tion was again manifested, we held
steady to our purpose and plan, snd the
years 1856.7 catalogued 187 pupils male
and foma'e, 91 white, 96 Colored. From
that to the present the numbers has

most of the time Colored and white

Colored in the ascending, but the white
now increasing in numbers. The aver-
ane age of publls sevemtecs
target: With our constitution thus drafted we purchased a tract of land as a site for our future college building and in the latter part of the year 1869, I went eastward to solicit funds with which to pay for our lands. During this absence the John Brown Raid came off and prominent friends of the church and school at Berea were driven out by mob violence. The war of the Rebellion came, and our college plans were suspended until that war was over. Speedily after the war our school was resumed and Colored pupils, by design, introduced. Opposition was again manifested, we held steady to our purpose and plan, and the years 1896-7 catalogued 187 pupils male and female, 91 white, 96 Colored. From that to the present the numbers has ranged from two to four hundred; and most of the time Colored and white have been nearly equal in number, the Colored in the ascending, but the white now increasing in numbers. The average age of pupils seventeen.

The Negro need not necessarily lose any more sleep over politics until he better's his material condition. Wealth and intelligence are going to control the destinies of this country, because it is right. What the race wants to do is to spend itself in an industrious effort to build upon wealth and character. Politics may not add these qualities, but these qualities can and will bring about political recognition.
Atlanta Independent.

target: Several days ago at St. Petersburg, Fla., a dog catcher went into the home of a respectable colored family and fought the wife, and her two daughters because they refused to let him take their dog from their own premises. target: A Fair is a place to show what is being produced. It is where we go to get an idea of the progress being made by our neighbors. We compare results and study how to accomplish greater things. The entertainment feature is very necessary to relieve the monotony, but the prime object should be to stimulate the people to make greater strides along material lines. Tennessee is on trial. The country is looking this way. The natural resources of this commonwealth are well-known to all, and it is a matter for us to decide whether we will develop them and enjoy the fruits therefrom or whether we will sit idly by and allow the foreigner to come in and develop them and reap the harvest. It goes without saying that the laborer will reap the harvest.

target: During the winter, of course, we had been giving the best attention possible to our ponies and dogs. We were a very small community, and the extent and success of our work was dependent as much on the condition of the animals as on the fitness of the men themselves. The first step was to find out what weight the ponies were actually capable of pulling, and to this end the sledges were loaded with known weights, and the ponies exercised up and down a measured two miles on the sea-ice. Every day, except when the blizzards stopped us, they were taken out, and they were soon in good, hard condition.

target: Keep Away From the Police Stations. Would it not be just as well if those of our race who persist in being the performers at the matinees held every Sunday morning in the Western and Northwestern Police Stations, would hold up a little and let the hard-working officials of the Police Department and the Department of Justice have a little breathing spell? At least until the election is over. It will no doubt be a great pleasure to the Police Justices who set in these two stations to give the Negroes who come before them 30 and 60 days in jail, and thus deprive them of the privilege of voting on the second day of November, because both of them have been given their places by the powers-that-be, and to keep them they must get rid of the Negro. At least that is what they profess to believe.

target: To the Nashville Globe: Once in a while, as we journey along life's highway, there come to us some opportunities in which we can be of service to others by our developing and making the best possible use of those opportunities. If we make fitting preparation to do our individual work in the great providential scheme of things to the best of our native and acquired ability, and if we cheerfully and assiduously apply ourselves to the performance of life's tasks, then we will have the satisfaction of knowing that we have contributed something by our humble though faithful efforts to the sum total of human happiness.

target: We arrived at the conclusion that the best results would be secured if 650 pounds per pony were made the maximum weight to be carried on the sledges, this weight including the weight of the sledges themselves, which I have already mentioned was about 60 pounds each. Some alterations were made in the motor-car in order to reduce its weight, all superfluous gear being taken off; and its performances on the sea-ice were so satisfactory that we had high hopes for the future. target: When the Negroes of the United States learned the glad fact that they were free and could go about their own business as other men, thousands of them set out to do something for themselves. Many of them did well, but a sad feature of it is too few of the sons are doing as well as their fathers did. We should begin now to rectify that fault. Every boy ought to be taught to do the kind of work his father does, and let him decide after he is grown whether he will continue to do that kind of work.

target: Henson's part in the Peary expedition looms up ever larger. target: Horneman, Pinkard, Robin, and particularly my excellent friend Clarkson, who as well as Wilberforce, is immortalized by his works and his zeal in the defence of Africans. George Roberts, an English navigator, pillaged by the captain of a privateer belonging to his country, sought refuge in the isle of St. John, in the archipelago, near Cape Vert. The Negroes give him succour. An anonymous pamphleteer, who dare not deny the fact, endeavors to extenuate its merit, by saying that the condition of George Roberts would have moved a tiger to pity. Durand extols the modesty and chastity of negro wives, and the good education of the mulattoes at Goree. Wadstrom, who boasts much of their friendship, thinks their sensibility more mild and affecting than those of the whites. Captain Wilson, who lived among them, speaks highly of their constancy in friendship; they shed tears at his departure.

in its infancy. They have generally preserved
an estimable simplicity of domestic 'manners.
They are distinguished by their tenderness for
their parents, and their great respect for the aged—
a patriarchal virtue, which, in our days, is almost
unknown.

target: Now, what would be the effect of this avowed principle of Mr. Breckinridge, endorsed "in every respect" by Governor Wickliffe, against the extension of slavery, if it were adopted, not as a Democratic measure alone, but as a Southern measure? The result would follow inevitably that no additional slave territory could ever be acquired. Texas, for instance, would not be allowed a division of its domain into other States with slavery. Cuba would never be annexed with other than Freesoil privileges, and the absolute end would be the restriction of all slave territory within the geographical limits now defined by the South.

target: The endorsement of the doctrine of the non-extension of slavery by Gov. Wickliffe, under the force of the dilemma in which he was placed by the Democratic candidate for the Vice-Presidency, has driven him to the most miserable sophistry to escape the suspicion of hostility to Southern institutions. "He fully endorses the speech of the Hon. J. C. Breckinridge in every respect." Yet, he directly proceeds to qualify this broad assertion by another proposition, "that he is not connected with a party which desires the interposition of the strong arm of the General Government for the extension of slavery."

correct: The Supreme Court is also “the strong arm of
the General Government As the friends of
Governor Wickliffe—the anti-slavery extension
Democrats—have taken care that all the territory
of Louisiana north of thirty-six degrees and thirty
minutes shall not be reinstated as slave teritory
by the revival of its ancient laws upon the repeal
of the Missouri Compromise, the slave holder.
who enters it, must look for the protection of his
property to the decision, by the Supreme Court, of
the question whether his slave continues to be
property, or loses that character by his emigra-
tion. 1 the Supreme Court decides in favor o
the slaveholder, “the strong arm of the Govern.
ment is used for the practical extonsion of sla-
very. But Governor Wickliffe and his friends do
do not desire this interference in favor of the cxten-
n
target: The Supreme Court is also "the strong arm of the General Government." As the friends of Governor Wickliffe—the anti-slavery extension Democrats—have taken care that all the territory of Louisiana north of thirty-six degrees and thirty minutes shall not be reinstated as slave territory by the revival of its ancient laws upon the repeal of the Missouri Compromise, the slave holder, who enters it, must look for the protection of his property to the Supreme Court, on the question whether his slave continues to be property, or loses that character by his emigration. If the Supreme Court decides in favor of the slaveholder, "the strong arm of the Government" is used for the practical extension of slavery. But Governor Wickliffe and his friends do not desire this interference in favor of the extension of slavery.

target: I therefore, take direct issue against Gov. Wickliffe and Mr. Breckinridge's acknowledged anti-Southern doctrines involved in the declaration that he belonged to no party which had for its "object the extension of slavery," and I declare that whatever favor it may find in the Democratic party, it finds none in the American party of which I am an humble member. The American party, on the contrary, favors the extension of slavery as a necessary national institution, protected by the Federal Government under the aegis of our Constitution and laws. We assume a national position on all national questions, demanding alike the fostering care of the National Government over the free institutions of the North and the slave institution of the South. We are essentially a national party, without sectional prejudices or preferences and have only the whole country and the Union at heart.

target: The South looks to the certainty of the future annexation of slave territory in its progress Southward, to fulfil its destiny. Armed interposition may be necessary to enforce our rights or our national honor. If in the conflict of arms, in a legitimate and lawful war, slave territory should, by chance, be acquired, it will not change its condition to that of free territory, for Governor Wickliffe does not desire "the strong (military and naval) arm of the Government to be exerted for the extension of slavery." If this be not the meaning of Governor Wickliffe, he means nothing, and attempts by the obscurity of language to escape the consequences of his fatal doctrine. If this be not a surrender of all the South claims, we know not what submission is.

target: Again we greet our readers with words of good cheer. Were it not for the known character of the writer of the following cheering letter, we should hesitate in publishing it as it is written. But he is one of us of New Orleans; he is a man of strict integrity—a man of business capacity—a cool, dispassionate observer of events, not led away by the impulses of enthusiasm. For his mercantile transactions and his connection with the great enterprises of the day, he is better known than as a politician. More than all, he was until lately a Democrat of the old school, who struggled for that good old cause when the banner of the party was born aloft by such heroes as Jackson of the iron will and patriotic heart.

target: STEAMER J. MONTGOMERY, Oct. 8, 1856. Editors of the Crcle: I have not language to describe to you the rush to the Fillmore ranks—all over the West since the Whig Convention at Baltimore. I have been stumping Illinois for two months, in the good cause. I have fought every inch of ground on this boat. We took the vote and it stood thus: Buchanan, 17; Fillmore, 40. On the Kennett out of about 200 passengers all were for Fillmore, but about six. While our boat was taking in cotton at Memphis, we had the largest kind of a Fillmore meeting in the public square. Do what you please with this imperfectly written communication.

target: NICARAGUA.—A love of adventure as well as a desire to enter into a field of enterprise ripe for the harvest, have given rare attractions to Central America. The sudden creation of a stable government where disorder and revolution had run riot—the immense importance of this narrow highway of future commerce between Asia and the civilized world, and the deliciousness of the climate and the fertility of the soil are strong inducements, indeed, to emigration. Those who desire to enter into the harvest field, have an opportunity now of doing so, free of expense, by calling upon Col. Mancosas, corner of Common and St. Charles streets. A vessel starts on the 27th of each month.

correct: It is a truism that the Federal Government can
have no power either to extend or abolish sla-
very. No one doubts this. Itis the Calhoun
docirine, while it is equally admitted that, unde
the Constitution, the Federal Government must

target: It is a truism that the Federal Government can have no power either to extend or abolish slavery. No one doubts this. It is the Calhoun doctrine, while it is equally admitted that, under the Constitution, the Federal Government must protect slavery wherever it exists. Besides this, it is equally insisted upon that no territorial power can restrict its extension within its own borders, while under the control of the General Government. These principles are the admitted axioms of the South, against which no Southern man dare to raise his voice if he be devoted to our peculiar institutions under the determined rights of the South as established by the Constitution.

There are stories innumerable of the manner in which the reptiles charm the feathered creatures and end by swallowing them at a gulp. But there is one bird that is not susceptible to the fascination of his snakeship's eye, and that is the common blue jay, found in the woods all over the United States, says the Chicago Chronicle.

target: A gentleman who has lived for many years in the country asserts positively that the blue jay can speak at least as plainly as a human being, and that word is "snake." "When I was a boy," he said recently, "I killed many snakes that would have escaped but for the sharp eyes of the blue jay. Some species of reptiles will climb small trees and bushes and trap their prey in that manner. They feed on small birds principally. But they never catch a jaybird. He is the snake's enemy for the whole bird family. As soon as a blue jay sees a snake he sounds the alarm. You can hear him squall a quarter of a mile, and he articulates the word 'snake' as plainly as I can. He will hop about on a limb and yell 'snake! snake!' in such a thrill, excited voice that pretty soon others of his tribe hasten to the scene, and all join in giving the alarm. All other birds, hearing this warning cry, fly away, but the blue jay splits his throat with cries until his enemy has disappeared.

Dr. Stone was a strong advocate of female suffrage.

target: A snake once caught a blue jay by charming him. The jay yelled 'Snake! snake!' at the top of his voice. Several hundred blue jays flew to his rescue and pecked the snake's eyes out, and finally picked him to pieces, thus saving the life of their companion. Ever since that time the blue jays, on seeing a snake, gives the alarm and all blue jays within hearing will hasten to the scent and lend their voices to the warning. This may sound incredible, but it's true," said the narrator in conclusion. "Not at all, but it's a good story to forget under the circumstances," added the nervous young man, who proposed the consideration of the action of the jouncing grape to the doctrine of metempsychosis.

target: Unexpected frankness now and then gives a special zest to the humor of a situation in congress. When "Gabe" Bouck was the representative from the Oshkosh district of Wisconsin a pension bill came before him; for, to his great vexation of spirit; for, while his personal sympathies were directly opposed to it, his political interests were strong enough to whip him into line. On the day the bill came up for final disposal a fellow member met Bouck in the space behind the last row of seats, walking back and forth and gesticulating excitedly, bringing his clenched right fist down into the hollow of his left hand, to the accompaniment of expletives which would literally look well in print, writes Francis E. Leupp in "Some Humors of Congress".

Wamsley claimed that science would probably lose more by breaking into his course of study than justice would gain by his jury service and he was accordingly excused from serving on the jury.

target: which have been beneficial to all the people throughout the city. He has succeeded in having more miles of asphalt pavement laid in the 20th ward than any other ward in the city. In view of this fact and the further fact that he has been especially attentive to the needs and the wants of the people of his ward, maintaining and office in the ward and meeting the people each day in the week, in appreciation of his good work in their behalf, they, the people, named after him "Patterson Park," which is bounded by Leavitt, DeKalb and Boone streets. In every way Alderman Patterson is greatly interested in beautifying Chicago. He is a director of the Industrial Art League, a prominent member of the Municipal Art League, President of the Lincoln Club, member of the Illinois and Monokon Clubs, actively connected with the "K. of P.," Royal Arcanum, Royal League, National Union, and other secret societies and it goes without saying that he will succeed himself in the City Council after the 7th of April.

target: King Edward has been restricted by his physicians to five cigars a day. He has been an inveterate smoker almost since his boyhood. Some 30 years ago he was dining with the late Lord Derby, who regarded the use of tobacco as a vulgar and unpleasant habit. After dinner the prince suggested a cigar, whereupon his lordship expressed regret that his house did not contain a smoking-room, and that he could only suggest the stables as a suitable place for burning tobacco. Much to his surprise the prince adjourned to the region indicated and enjoyed his postprandial cigar.

target: The taking profit or commission for the sale or exchange of goods and also the wage system or the buying and selling of labor and products of labor for money. Socialism would abolish all of these things and institute in their place a system of free exchange of labor and the products of labor. And by giving everyone the right to do their share of the labor would give everyone a full share of the world's stock of goods, most probably using the hour as the unit to measure labor performed, so it is plain that our issue is not "free baths" or "free soup," but free men and free labor and the free exchange of labors products. target: Mrs. Lucille Elliott Weber was appointed by City Clerk Olson to assist in making the new registration of voters. A white lady prominent in local religious and welfare circles received a similar appointment. At first the duties of the ladies kept them apart. Later Mr. Olson assigned them to counter duty, working side by side. The Christian white lady told Mr. Olson she "could not stomach" the idea of working beside a colored woman. Mr. Olson told her he did not like that brand of religion, and that she could quit if she wished, as Mrs. Weber was giving satisfaction and was apt to remain awhile. The "nice Christian" lady resigned. Mrs. Weber is still at her desk.

target: Food that had been canned for six years was opened recently and tested by a Dutch physician, Van Leersum. It was a meat hash, including onions and potatoes, and the doctor's conclusion was that the food was quite suitable for human consumption. The essential thing in canning, Doctor Van Leersum decided, was that all the air should be exhausted from the can in sealing. Oxygen, he said, was responsible for any corrosion that occurred.

target: (N. A. C. P. Press Service) New York, Nov. 12.—Serge Koussevitzky, Conductor of the Boston Symphony Orchestra and one of the leading orchestral conductors of the East, is to conduct a chamber orchestra concert in the Town Hall on November 27, at which will be performed a musical setting composed by Louis Gruenberg for James Weldon Johnson's poem, "The Creation." This will constitute the first performance in America of the work. Other compositions of leading European composers are also to have a first American performance on this occasion, the composers represented including the Russian composer, Igor Stravinsky, and Bela Bartok.

target: The Freeman Representative, B. T. Tanner, D. D., was in the city this and last weekend and we appreciated his presence very much. John Phillips is expected in next week. An excellent programme was rendered last Sunday evening at 7:30 by the Endeavor Association. The choir was of the best singers of the city and Walter Young deserves great credit for his excellent cornet music. Mrs. S. V. Lowery presided at the organ. M. G. Brooks is expected home soon. Minor Bradshaw is on the sick list. Mrs. S. Nukolls is on the sick list. We are very sorry to know that some untruth teller has tried to disgrace one of our very popular young ladies, who are respected by all persons in Paducah in the person of Miss Margurite Tandy. And if that untruth teller is found he will have a warm reception. Ezekiel Grundy is controller of John Carr's grocery and wishes to see his friends over there. Please pay the press, or you can't have the paper, and it takes money to run business.

More than half the pain and dissomfort of
ordinary lives comes from the petty unkind-
ness and slights among kindred and friends.
More than half the wrecks of what were once
promising friendships are caused, not by the
disoovery of one or the other of great defici-
encies in mind or character in the friend,
but by petty trick or deceptions revealed, or
emall negleots, or those slights hardest of ail
to pardon, andnever, we fear, quite forgotten
that are done, not to one's sel, but to the
near and dear ones, whose feelings we would
sate guard at any time at the expense of our
own. Be kind in time. Be kind evenatthe
risk of personal inconventence. Be kind td
the intirm and old and uninteresting, Be
kind to the people you don't like. Forget
the irritating word. Send your thought just
asecond or two ehead of your speech, that
want of tact may not effeot as much mischief
as positive ill will
target: Excellent Thoughts for Young People. More than half the pain and discomfort of ordinary lives comes from the petty unkindness and slights among kindred and friends. More than half the wrecks of cause, not by the discovery of one or the other of great deficiencies in mind or character in the friend, but by petty trick or deceptions revealed, or small neglects, or those slights hardest of all to pardon, and never, we fear, quite forgotten—that are done, not to one’s self, but to the near and dear ones, whose feelings we would safeguard at any time at the expense of our own. Be kind in time. Be kind even at the risk of personal inconvenience. Be kind to the infirm and old and uninteresting. Be kind to the people you don’t like. Forget the irritating word. Send your thought just a second or two ahead of your speech, that want of tact may not effect as much mischief as positive ill will.

target: The nose was re-enamelled 16 times before the artist was satisfied that he had matched exactly the woman's complexion. As the patient's skin was dark and reddish and full of color it was difficult to imitate. Finally, however, when the nose was finished, it was as natural as could be—darkest at the roots, lightest at the bridge and on the tip, and it rose out of the face instead of resting against it. Now the woman has a better shaped nose than she ever had before and, besides, the most durable one.—New York Herald.

target: On Tuesday Dec 29, Mrs. Leah Reed, at her beautiful residence on Pearl St., entertained a large number of relatives and friends at dinner at the hour of 2:30 p. m. Her daughters, Mesdames Lottie Halleck, and Mary Wright, made it pleasant for every one. Mrs. Halleck, favored the guest with beautiful selections on the instrument. Several relatives from St. Louis, East St. Louis, and her sister, Mrs. Cora Burton, and two children of Paducah, Ky., were present. Several from Belgrade, and Metropolis, of course Old Rambler, was there.

We very much regret to have to reply to this part of Dr. Fisher's article, but it is he who has drawn the fire and endure the flames; but the Dr. has yet to learn that no such logic turns a student pale but holds eel of argument by the tail. Brother Fisher, here is said of Brother Phillips by those who have known us longest.

target: Rev. C. C. Phillips, Golconda, Ill. Dear Brother, and Colaborer, I write to say I thru the Gazette that your Institute and Executive Board held a meeting in which a resolution was passed advising all of the churches of the East Mt. Olive Association to cooperate with your State Convention. That in my judgment was one of the most timely resolutions ever passed in the State of Illinois. I know you must have been that thoughtful, author, it is just like you to take time by the forelock, and do such things. May the blessings of God abide with you. I truly wish that every church, S. S. and Mission Circle and B. Y. P. U. in our great State would do just what you have advised your association to do.

target: HEN Jesus said to his disciples that they must take up their cross daily and follow him. He was borrowing a term from his own experience and transferring it to ours. "The cross" meant to Jesus the sacrifices demanded by the duties which the will of God laid on him. Jesus came to earth willingly, but not on his own initiative: "The Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world." And he came with just one purpose: "I came not to do mine own will, but the will of him that sent me." A part of the will was that he should die, in order that we, through his death, might have life. He spoke of himself as the Good Shepherd who was to lay down his life for the sheep, and added: "This commandment have I received of my Father." Therefore he endured the cross, despising the shame. When he speaks to us of the every-day cross, Jesus can only be referring to experiences in daily life which have for us the same significance the cross of Calvary had for him.

Sometimes the duties of one of these relationships absorb the whole of life, making it one long agony of sacrifice, lightened only by love. Perhaps a mother is an invalid. Perhaps a father has been unfortunate in business and grown utterly discouraged. Sometimes a brother or sister has gone wrong, or a child turned out a source of disappointment instead of pride. The whole life of some healthy and normal member of the family has to be spent in caring for, encouraging or seeking to reclaim the unfortunate one.

FIRING A FOURTH OF JULY SALUTE
It must be admitted that the practice has undergone some changes with the passing of the years. In the days of our grandfathers the firing of the big guns kept up pretty well all day—indeed in these days the citizens of the new republic liked it so well that they fired off cannon not only on the Fourth, but on Thanksgiving Day and New Year's as well. And Independence Day if the booming did not keep up all day at least there was a salute at sunrise, another at noon. The gunners grew tired or the powder was exhausted and when the volume of each peal of artificial thunder varied according to the guesswork of the amateurs in measuring out the powder.

target: There are some who bear the cross bravely, proudly, yet bitterly. That statement makes one think of Dante. He loved Florence as few have ever loved the city of their birth; and yet for nineteen years before his death in 1321 he was compelled to wander from one city of Italy to another, perhaps as far as Paris and Oxford, with a death sentence registered against him by the city of his love. Such pride and scorn as he was capable of, however, have rarely had residence in a human breast. Rossetti, himself the son of an Italian exile, has given a picture of the wandering poet which may stand for many a burdened soul today, whose cross galls, whose heart hath no rest, whose spirit has grown bitter, but who disdains to try to escape the cross, unless they can do so on their own terms.

target: In this more decorous generation we have to be content, most of us, with one salute of this sort on the Fourth. At U. S. navy yards and military posts and other governmental reservations the salute is fired at noon, but in the average town or city where the civilians must do the firing—especially if these self-made civilians are to participate in a picnic or a parade—the regulation salute is sent echoing over the countryside soon after the break of day. Of course, if there is to be a ceremonial flag raising during the day, or a monument is to be unveiled or some high-up public official is to be given a reception, there may be a salute as a sort of accompaniment for the function, but for the most part nowadays the average American community has to get along on the Fourth with one formal salute.

target: The Fourth of July salutes for noise making was genuine fun for the men who did it years ago, whereas it is no more nor less than a detail of the day's work for Uncle Sam's gunners. Indeed it may surprise some of our readers to learn that there are artillerymen in our regular army and men-o'-war's-men on our naval vessels who thoroughly detest the roar of the big guns. It is not that they are afraid or are lacking in experience, for some of these men have been in the service for years and have repeatedly faced death in a variety of forms and yet a veteran never gets over his dislike of the din at close range.

target: A salute should consist of a specific number of discharges having a certain significance, and one or another of these regulation salutes are fired when the noise-making is in the hands of regular or volunteer soldiery, but independent gun crews recruited for the Fourth continue to claim extensive license in this respect. There are several different salutes as prescribed in Uncle Sam's books of regulations that may be adopted for the Fourth of July greeting. Perhaps the most extensively used of all is the American salute of one gun for every state in the Union. By allowing an interval of a minute and a half between discharges this salute can be strung out for more than an hour and at half-minute intervals, which is slow enough to suit most persons, it enables an interval of booming that exceeds twenty minutes.

target: Another change that has come with the passing of the years is in the matter of the arrangements for the firing of the salutes. The average individual who listens to the reverberation of the distant guns never gives a thought to this part of the celebration, but obviously somebody has to attend to this part of the celebration. In the old days almost anybody who volunteered for the task—and who, mayhap, was willing to contribute for the powder—was considered competent to act as artilleryman for the occasion. Similarly almost anything that would answer the purpose of a cannon was accepted as a vehicle of noise making. How often under such circumstances was a worn old fieldpiece dragged forth to do such duty and loaded nigh to the muzzle by enthusiasts who thought far more of the din than of the danger that lay in an overcharge of ammunition. Sometimes a hollow log or even a length of pipe was made to serve as a substitute.

target: A third class of people try to bear their cross as Jesus bore his. As he said, "Take up the cross daily, and follow me," he meant "Follow me in the bearing of the cross." The experience of Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane is the key to his attitude toward the cross. The burden of what was before him seemed heavier than he could bear; but when his vision was cleared from the obscuring clouds of momentary depression and horror, and he saw unmistakably that Calvary was God's will for him, he arose and went out to meet it. What the cross of Calvary was to Jesus the everyday cross may be made to us, by the simple expedient of taking it up and following Jesus in the bearing of it.

target:
[Communicated.]
WARTING.—Very seldom do we find an incumbent of a public office writing in a plain, legible hand. We have, however, met with one at last, who writes so plain that whoever runs can read. It is neither more nor less a personage than the new Mayor. He sent us a notice yesterday, notifying that a Complaint Book would be opened on the 1st of July, and that citizens can call and write down their complaints of whatever nature, relating to the police and general government of the city. There is no mistake in the writing. It is plain, bold, compact and vigorous. Our compositors will have no difficulty whatever is making out the intent and purport. Hereafter, in the competency of a candidate for public office, we shall consider it a test, can he write well? If others of our public officers will improve their hand-writing, they will save a vast deal of trouble to the inmates of a printing office.—Crescent.

target: Nil mortuis nisi bonum, say nothing of the death except to praise, is a maxim that had its origin in noble feeling, but it does not inculcate the duty of praising where it is undeserved. The ignoble, unworthy dead, hence, pass into speedy oblivion, their evil deeds and themselves like together.—But when even enemies praise, and labor to polish their eulogies on the departed, it is a concession of merit—an acknowledgment of virtues of no ordinary character.

target: To-day, the revolving hours bring round again the Anniversary of our national birth. Unlike other nations, we can look back upon every incident of our eventful history. There is no misty triditionary period to give gigantic proportions to any event, or add to the heroic deeds or the exalted characters of the founders of our republic. Yet what nation ever gloried in nobler men—even boasted of brighter annals—ever made the record of greater progress?
W. SCOTT SHERMAN, Formerly of the Picayune Job Office, latterly of the firm of Sherman, Wharton & Co.

target: To-day the nation, whose flag, sixty-nine years ago shivered even under the tomahawk of the arrow of the Indian, commands the respect of the world. The people, whose territories then but skirted the Eastern slope of the Alleghanies, have spread from the Atlantic to the Pacific, from the lakes to the Gulf of Mexico. The institutions, which in the eyes of the nations hoary with age, were but as dreams, as unsubstantial and as ephemeral, exhibit a strength unknown to other forms of government, and exert an influence co-extensive with civilization. Our education for self government; the all-pervading sense of authority, controlling wills and passions, though it be not felt, and exhibits no external symbols of power, has been the peaceful agent in building up feeble states with such unexampled rapidity into a mighty nation.

target: Any other construction of the letter would render it entirely inconsistent with itself. Having urged the adoption of the Missouri Compromise, the inference is irresistible that Congress, in my opinion, possesses the power to legislate upon the subject of slavery in the territories. What an absurdity would it then be, if whilst asserting this SOVEREIGN POWER IN CONGRESS, which power, from its nature, must be EXCLUSIVE, I should in the very same breath also claim this identical power "for the population of a territory in an unorganized capacity"?

FILLMORE IN BROOKLYN.
The reception of Mr. Fillmore in Brooklyn on Wednesday, the twenty-fifth June, was truly imposing. The streets were filled with thousands; every window and balcony was crowded with beautiful women, whose admiration of their guest seemed to know no bounds. The warm and cordial reception in Brooklyn was if anything, a richer compliment, to the patriotic services and national character of Mr. Fillmore—than that which he received in New York. We have room only to append the reply Mr. Fillmore made to the Mayor, who welcomed him to the city as its honored guest: Mr. Fillmore said:

target: The Ladies Literary and Industrious Club was delightfully entertained, Thursday evening, by Mrs. Callie Ward, at her beautiful home on the corner of Ewing avenue and Bass street. While the ladies seemed to have been busy stitching in time on their various pieces of embroidery work, such as dollies, centerpieces, waists, pillow tops, a literary program was rendered. After which a short address on "Club work" was given by a Mrs. D. Huston. Other visitors to the club were Mrs. A. W. Whittaka, Gilbert White, A. J. Dodge, I. A. Nickerson and C. C. Canton. The ladies were invited to the dining-room, which was decorated with spring flowers and ferns, and a charming menu of two courses was served. The hostess was assisted by two members of the club, Mrs. Agnes Gentry and Sarah Ewing. Other members present were Mrs. Bettie Webb, Nellie Gouch, Annie V. Harris, Mrs. Turner.

This will bring us to a true and normal condition. I see clearly what the effect would be. Commercial disturbances would be the natural result, for it would be a great and radical change. The first effect would be to stop manufacturing here, and the country would be filled with foreign goods, many of which Europe would never see her money for. target: Already the papers of the country—those outspoken and influential tribunes of the people—have given their judgment clearly and unreservedly on the “one-term” idea, given immediate shape and agency by Mr. Sumner’s proposed amendment. With nearly one voice the entire press sustains the one-term principle, but refuses to have it pressed as a partisan move to affect any passing candidate. This common-sense view of the people, founded in honest feeling, squares with a fundamental principle of elemental law. No general rule is to be framed for the purpose of carrying a special case. Principles must be founded on justice and right, and not on party or personal feeling.

target: The country is now in the enjoyment of substantial prosperity under the existing tariff laws, which have been recently modified by a very large (in some instances, we think, injurious) reduction of duties, and by the transfer to the free list of many articles formerly paying duty. In its present form, while the tariff offers reasonable encouragement to most important home manufactures, it is in no sense prohibitory, for imports are large, and the power of the country to purchase and consume foreign products was never greater than at present. During the past year more mines have been opened, more factories erected, and more railroads constructed than ever before; business is upon a sound basis, every form of labor has been well rewarded, and the credit of the Government has so much improved that there has been a struggle between American and foreign bankers to obtain the five per cent. target: F. DONA, Esq., Lexington, Mo.: Mr Dear Sir: I have yours of the 14th in stant. I have no doubt that the policy you suggest is that which your party ought to adopt. They should have taken up Salmon P. Chase in 1868. Then, as the result of that contest, the return of genuine peace and thrift would have been promoted. That policy gave you more last year in Missouri than could have been achieved by a party triumph. You only err as to the proper candidate. I am not the man you need. Your party is mostly free trade, and I am not a ferocious protectionist. I have no doubt that I might be nominated and elected by your help, but it would place us all in a false position. If I, who am adversely interested, can see this, I am sure your good sense will on reflection realize it. You must take some man like Gratz Brown, or Trumbull, or General Cox, late Secretary of the Interior, and thus help to pacify and reunite our country anew.

target: But his statement that the press is everywhere of the same opinion, is not sustained by fact. The only papers which have come out squarely for the proposed amendment are those which are bent on the defeat of General Grant, by hook or by crook, without any reference to the will of the people, and have avowedly adopted it as the best means, in their opinion, of accomplishing that purpose. Their object is to operate upon public sentiment; and if they can so bring it to bear upon Congress as to secure a vote submitting the amendment to the people, they will use the fact to defeat, or with the hope of defeating, his nomination, or his election, if nominated. “Land!” It was a masterly effort, and he carried the house with storms of applause. The people are patiently awaiting the coming of D. Webster Davis, the poet, humorist and platform orator.

Hon. S. W. Starks, Supreme Chancellor K. of P., was in this city last Sunday, March 24th, and attended the 20th anniversary of Damon Lodge No. 3. The services were held at the Quinn Chapel A. M. E. Church. The Rev. Morrow preached the anniversary sermon. The church was crowded and the services were grand in every respect. The singing of the choir was charming, and the address of Supreme Chancellor was a full tide of eloquence, and remarkable for its wealth of Pythian success and progress. While here the Supreme Chancellor was highly entertained by the Kentucky “boys,” and after a business meeting with the Executive Committee of the Encampment, he left being highly pleased with his trip to Old Kentucky. Mr. Starks says he regards the Freeman as one of the best papers in the country.

input:
correct: Maternal duties, the nobility of
soul and the beauty of a charmins
personality, both in the home and in
society. Another great lesson the
commingling of club life can teach is
that of raising from the ordinary to
the level of the true and ideal woman
capable of realizing that though the
duties of men and women differ, the
status of both in the eyes of the law
and society should be the same and
that nothing should be required of
one that is not required of the other.
For instance, let us demand that men
continue to resard chastity as being
indispensable in women, bu. let wom.
en require the same from men and
let her, if modest, regard as a pro-
found insult the insinuation of any
whose power of appreciating her pur-
ity has been destroyed by careless
and indifferent living. I emphasize
this as the only means of lifting our
women up to the position required
where she can hold up a banner for
hon
target: Maternal duties, the nobility of soul and the beauty of a charming personality, both in the home and in society. Another great lesson the commingling of club life can teach is that of raising from the ordinary to the level of the true and ideal womanhood, by realizing that though the duties of men are provided for, the status of women in both the eyes of the law and society should be the same and that nothing should be required of one that is not required of the other. For instance, let us demand that men continue to regard chastity as being indispensable in women, but let women require the same from men and let her, if modest, regard as a profound insult the insinuation of any whose power of appreciating her purity has been destroyed by careless and indifferent living. I emphasize this as the only means of lifting our women up to the position required, where she can hold up a banner for a race so sadly in need of one.

target: Atlanta, Ga., July 17.—The Negroes of Georgia are a thrifty people. They are always on the hunt for property, education and every other good thing. They fight and love each other but seem to get there just the same. When the time for united action arrives, they are sure to get together in order to see that the right thing is done at the right time. They are always active in the summer time and particularly in the month of July. The grand lodges meet about that time and the political groups get together for good or evil. The political activities of the Negroes of Georgia are mainly centered in this lodge and church conferences.

input:
correct: The Negroes of Atlanta have been
in a state of excitement during the
past few weeks over the killing of
several of the women of the race.
The criminal has not yet been caught
but the colored people are manifest-
ing unusual activity by conducting
prayer and resolution meetings. Bv-
ery church, denomination and ereed
seems to be getting in the lime light.
it is hard to tell just where the thing
will end since the Negroes have al-
ready decided along with the “white
folks” that the one committing the
the deeds is a Negro, before the crim-
inal has either been caught or seen
or any evidence has been produced
to prove that a Negro is to be held
responsible for the killing of so many
of the women of his race. They are
chasing around signing petitions and
resolutions like a lot of wild geese
by a hungry Jew.
target: The Negroes of Atlanta have been in a state of excitement during the past few weeks over the killing of several of the women of the race. The criminal has not yet been caught but the colored people are manifesting unusual activity by conducting prayer and resolution meetings. Every church, denomination and creed seems to be getting in the lime light. It is hard to tell just where the thing will end since the Negroes have already decided along with the “white folks” that the one committing the deeds is a Negro, before the criminal has either been caught or seen or any evidence has been produced to prove that a Negro is to be held responsible for the killing of so many of the women of his race. They are chasing around signing petitions and resolutions like a lot of wild geese pursued by a hungry Jew.

nent politicians of the party, is that

fice to make two campaigns, one be-
fore the selection of delegates and
another after the delegates had bees
selected. It ts known that a number

the Ogden resolution were put in ef-
fect by the primary plan. in this
manner the candidates could go be-
fore the people and allow the voters

target: One of the main objections to the district unit in the selection of delegates to a state convention, as expressed by some of the more prominent politicians of the party, is that it would require candidates for office to make two campaigns, one before the selection of delegates and another after the delegates had been selected. It is known that a number of the leaders would be satisfied if the Ogden resolution were put in effect by the primary plan. In this manner the candidates could go before the people and allow the voters to express their choice and the candidates receiving the majority of the votes in a Magisterial District would be entitled to the delegates from that district in a state convention.

target: Washington, July 18.—The situation in Haiti is causing grave anxiety to American Minister Furniss, who reported to the state department today that the revolution in spite of temporary government victories, appears to be growing. Heretofore the revolutionists had confined their operations to the interior and small towns on the northern coast, Mr. Furniss reports, but they have now seized several more important points among them the second city in the republic, Gonaives, where large quantities of supplies are stored belonging to an American railway construction company. A late telegram from the minister today suggested the advisability of sending another American warship to Haitian waters and the gunboat Des Moines, now at Boston, is under orders to proceed there at once.

ver in Omaha. Fortunately as yet,
fery few of our people have been af-
ficted with this dangerous disease.
Too much precaution, however, cannot
be used. In the case of illness, do not
be too quick to jump to the conclu-
sion that it is only a bad cold or la
grippe, particularly if there be sore
throat and a fever. Call one of our
doctors. Be on the safe side. “Safety

rst is a good slogan at such a time
s this. Never be guilty of the crime
of concealing a contagious disease. To
do so is both eriminal and wicked, It
s the duty of every good citizen to
conserye the health of the community.
target: There is a great deal of scarlet fever in Omaha. Fortunately, however, very few of our people have been afflicted with this dangerous disease. Too much precaution, however, cannot be used. In the case of illness, do not be too quick to jump to the conclusion that it is only a bad cold or la grippe, particularly if there be sore throat and a fever. Call one of our doctors. Be on the safe side. "Safety first" is a good slogan at such a time as this. Never be guilty of the crime of concealing a contagious disease. To do so is both criminal and wicked. It is the duty of every good citizen to conserve the health of the community.

target: Our attention has been called to the fact that in our editorial in last week's issue on “A Significant Action”, which has received most favorable comment, we failed to mention the State Bank of Omaha, as one of the local banks which employs a colored man in a responsible position. We regret the error and hasten to correct it. The State Bank from the start has employed a messenger, who like the men employed in the Omaha National and the United States National, has given good satisfaction. In making this correction we desire to state that The Monitor aims to be both fair and accurate and that therefore we shall be glad to have our attention called to any error appearing in our columns.

input:
correct: When the Liberty Bell on its re-
turn trip from the Pacifie coast stop-
ped at Fort Worth, the school chil-
dren were permitted to gather around
the old relic, touch it with their hands,
and kiss it with their lips, and then it
bappened. It was a frightful thing
to happen in a civilied community
and no wonder the people were
thrown into a panie and narrowly
escaped riot and bloodshed. A Negro
school-child tried to kiss the bell
That settled it. The mob spirit was
aroused in that civilized white com
munity as if by an electrie shock and
instantly the blood of ignorant fanat-
es was fired and they threatened to
drive every colored man out of the
ity. For shame! When I think of
that Negro child blush scarlet for
my race. If the kiss of that child
was an imsult to that community then
the Liberty Bell had no business
there. The first blood shed in the
war of the revolution which ended in
the independence proclaimed by the
old bell was the blood of a marty
with a coal-black skin
target: When the Liberty Bell on its return trip from the Pacific coast stopped at Fort Worth, the school children were permitted to gather around the old relic, touch it with their lips, and kiss it with their lips, and then it happened. It was a frightful thing to happen in a civilized community and no wonder the people were thrown into a panic and narrowly escaped riot and bloodshed. A Negro school-child tried to kiss the bell. That settled it. The mob spirit was aroused in that civilized white community as if by an electric shock and instantly the blood of ignorant fanatics was fired and they threatened to drive every colored man out of the city. For shame! When I think of that Negro child I blush scarlet for my race. If the kiss of that child was an insult to that community then the Liberty Bell had no business there. The first blood shed in the war of the independence proclaimed by the old bell was the blood of a martyr with a coal-black skin.

There was a prominent firm of
butchers, who were influential mem-
bers of the Woodmen of the World.
They had a large trade, almost a
monopoly of the trade of the Colored
people of that Ohio town. Suddenly
this trade fell off. Members of the
firm began to inquire the reason for
it. They knew that the Colored peo-
ple had not suddenly become vegetar-
lans and must be still cating meat.
When they learned that they were be-
ing boycotted because they were in-
fuential members of the W. O. W.
who owned the opera house building
where they had begun segregating
the Colored people, these influential
merchants “got busy” and in less than
ten days the Colored people were
told that they might have seats, as
formerly, wherever they wanted to
buy them in that house.
target:
Miss Blank plainly showed her indignation, but replied quietly, "No, sir, I don't blame you. I shall not, however, submit to such an arbitrary rule. Fortunately, I don't have to see the play. I shall not buy a ticket."

There was a prominent firm of butchers, who were influential members of the Woodmen of the World. They had a large trade, almost a monopoly of the trade of the Colored people of that Ohio town. Suddenly this trade fell off. Members of the firm began to inquire the reason for it. They knew that the Colored people had not suddenly become vegetarians and must still be eating meat. When they learned that they were being boycotted because they were influential members of the W. O. W. who owned the opera house building where they had begun segregating the Colored people, these influential merchants "got busy" and in less than ten days the Colored people were told that they might have seats, as formerly, wherever they wanted to buy them in that house.

target: a small nationality in our own South had been crushed at a great expense of blood and treasure. The entire tendency of modern civilization was toward large nationalities and strong governments. It was difficult for a small nationality to maintain a position of respectability. Delaware would make a very respectable county, scarcely a respectable State, but as an independent nation she would be simply ridiculous, yet it was expected of San Domingo to set up as a nation on an equally small capital. For his part he had no wish to be a citizen of a meek nation that could be kicked about by every one with impunity. He wanted to get into large relationship, and was he a citizen of San Domingo, he would hold up both his hands from the rising to the setting of the tropical sun from annexation to the United States. He had thought of other nations, but could think of none except the United States with which as a citizen of San Domingo he should desire to cast in his lot.

target: He had come very willingly, but with very inadequate preparation to give his views upon the annexation of San Domingo. He alluded first to its relation to the Republican party, holding that however important the subject might be in itself, it was of far less importance than the ascendancy of this party in our own national affairs. He pictured briefly the situation of affairs in the Ku-Klux districts of the South, and asked, "If such things are done in the green tree what shall be done in the dry?" If the Constitution and laws of the United States cannot be enforced by an Administration friendly to their enforcement, what might we expect if the pro-slavery, disunion Democratic party should be again placed in power? This, then, he held was no time to put in jeopardy the ascendancy of the Republican party for San Domingo or any other object however desirable in itself.

target: Mr. Douglass was once opposed to extending the sway of the Government anywhere. That was when its power was used to uphold human bondage. But now he was in favor of extending it any and everywhere. He held that a nation had no right to withhold its succor and relief from those who needed aid when it was able to extend such succor. He saw no good reason why all the nations of the American continent should not one day be gathered under the wings of this Republic. He thought we had as yet no adequate conception of the expansion hereafter in store for us, and spoke of the aptitude for such a destiny which our complex Government, consisting of many States, independent for local purposes and retaining their peculiar characteristics, gave to our nation.

target: We are a community of hardy mechanics and laborers, with our wives and little ones looking to us for support. We ask these favors for the sake of the citizen who makes both sailor and soldier; and we urge it for all without discriminating against any. We ask it for the white man, because our interests in this particular are identical. And we beg it for the black man, for the reason that all his wages made in the Naval Department previous to the rebellion have been turned over to his master, leaving him nought to live upon when the war broke out but the atmosphere by which he was surrounded.

target: The examination at this institution, which commenced on Monday, the 22d, and is now going on, has shown that the earnest and untiring efforts of Professors Storum and King have been productive of great good. The students at this seminary are mostly young men over twenty years of age, who only very recently learned to read and write. On the day of our attendance we were more than surprised at the proficiency of these students in the studies of arithmetic, grammar, (English and Latin,) algebra, reading, geography, and church history. This school is under the care and supervision of the Baptist Home Mission Society, and is doing a good work in the way of dissipating that feeling of prejudice against colored people entertained by such white Baptists as declare their belief that the institution of slavery was the best possible academy for colored people. target: And I would say to your honor, in conclusion, that we are not here as advocates for any particular clique or party, outside of those who have accepted the situation; and with us have thrown themselves upon the progressive side, declaring it to be their duty to repair the damages of the past in accordance with the doctrine of reconstruction. And, sir, it is to be regretted that certain Government officials and others have failed to take a broad, comprehensive, and catholic view of this matter, rather than narrowing down the patronage of this Government to what is known as the “rings,” “cliques,” &c. And these the people have denounced, by large and overwhelming majorities, as no doubt your honor may have recently seen in the journals of our section.

target: During his stay on the island he met with about a dozen of the colony of colored people that went from Baltimore forty-seven years ago. There were sixteen of the original colonists still living, who had not only survived the climate, but all the revolutions that had swept over the country. The colony now numbered about seven hundred, retaining the English language and the Methodist religion—the latter such as we knew it in the good old times. Here, he said, we had got out of the old fashion, but these colonists still sing the good old tunes of long ago, and shout as Methodists used to do before they had colleges. The members of this colony are now anxiously awaiting annexation. They had heard of emancipation and the enfranchisement of their race, and of the efforts put forth for their education; and now that the old flag had become the symbol of freedom they were eager to get back under its folds.

target: While looking round his lot, he heard the sound of men and horns approaching. So, concealed himself, he wanted to see who and what they were. He was not kept long in suspense—looking out from his place of concealment, he observed twelve or fifteen men—some of them walking and some of them riding. They were all disguised. They went up to the house and demanded the door to be opened—his wife refused to do it, at the same time saying that Charles was not at home. The party burst open the door and rushed in, swearing that they had got the d—d scoundrel now, and swearing, also, what they intended doing with him. Charles thought now is the time to show fight. He fired off one of his gun barrels, bringing to the ground one of the marauders. The poor, unfortunate fellow bawled lustily for help, and made the night air hideous with his doleful cries, caused by the pain of his wound. Charles fired again—wounding another of these assassins. His ammunition being exhausted, he, like a good soldier, retreated.

target: A special meeting of the Medical Association of this District was held Tuesday, the 23d instant. The name of Dr. A. T. Augusta, a colored physician of this city, and late surgeon in the United States army with the rank of brevet lieutenant-colonel, was up for membership. He has proven by a rigid examination, made by surgeons whose prejudice against color was undoubted, that he is in every way qualified as a physician, so that there was no question as to his competency. He was, however, denied admission, and a resolution was passed forbidding members of the association from consulting with him on pain of dismissal from the association. Is this an evidence of the civilization, enlightenment, and progress of the 19th century?
